text:
LETTER FROM THE EXECUTIVE EDITOR
earingPoint’s Financial Services Technology Journalbrings you views on topics important to decision
makers in the financial services industry, presented in a way that puts technology in a true business context.
We believe that digitizationis one such issue and this issue is entirely dedicated to it.
Surely, the concept of digitization has been around forever: processing data in discrete binary digits is
what information technologists and businesses have been doing forever with “0s” and “1s” or their
equivalent encoded in vacuum-tube contraptions in times past or, most recently, in atomic quantum
states. (There is an hilarious Dilbert cartoon about an old-timer claiming that being able to use “0s” and
“1s” is an extravagance available only to the fortunate—and unappreciative—new generations.He goes
on saying, “When I started there were no 1s! I once coded an entire database entirely with 0s!”)
So what is so special about digitization today? Digitization is the momentous pattern that characterizes
what is happening in a wide range of domains in the financial industry: from portal-based claim
processing in insurance to straight-through process

doc_id: /Users/manuelbarbero/Developer/AIStudio/data/corpora/demo/uploads/FS Journal, Edited by M. Barbero - Digitization.pdf

text:
tization is the momentous pattern that characterizes
what is happening in a wide range of domains in the financial industry: from portal-based claim
processing in insurance to straight-through processing in capital markets to integrated commercial
loan underwriting in banking. As Richard Walker and Paul Dunay explain in the introductory article
“Digitization: The Time is Here,” digitization is about actively taking steps to implement an end-to-end
solution to support the full value chain, for better controls, standardization, consistency and measure-
ment of the business processes.
The actual underlying disciplines required to make digitization real (such as business process management,
content and document management, digital imaging, and workflow and collaboration software) have
themselves been around for a while, albeit not until recently with the level of performance or cost to
make them either ubiquitous or integrated the way financial institutions are starting to envision now.
And although individual tools supporting these disciplines enjoy double-digit licensing growth, by and
large, institutions are implementing these solutions in isolation.
Our point of view here is that a

text:
d although individual tools supporting these disciplines enjoy double-digit licensing growth, by and
large, institutions are implementing these solutions in isolation.
Our point of view here is that achieving the real value these tools offer requires a more encompassing
view and the one that we label digitizationprovides such a view, creating critical linkages between the
customer and the back office and among the disparate technologies now emerging. Digitization calls for
integrated vision and architectures with due focus on domains such as security and enterprise information
integration as well as the necessary computing and communications infrastructures.
To explore digitization in a structured way, we have organized the articles using a framework that we
prototyped in our inaugural publication and that we continue to use in this issue:
• The Business of Technology section explores how technologies create and deliver business value.
In “Digitization: The Time is Here,” Richard Walker and Paul Dunay describe how digitization
can transform financial institutions along the entire value chain of internal and external operations,
freeing up financial, human and information resources

The following article brings these concepts together immediately in a very focused manner—
In “Enterprise Credit Management: Digitization Comes of Age,” Bruce Klein and Sean Coyle
describe an approach to address and solve enterprise credit management, to enable financial services
firms to bring together all the pieces in the credit management chain. This piece shows how
digitization tenets help produce an integrated vision and architecture to create a seamless process that
speeds loan closings and significantly improves the efficiency of all personnel involved in the credit
management process.
• How It (Should) All Startexamines the new approaches to strategy and architecture that are shaping
the information technology world—In this section, we explore in more detail some of the disciplines
that make di

text:
uld) All Startexamines the new approaches to strategy and architecture that are shaping
the information technology world—In this section, we explore in more detail some of the disciplines
that make digitization possible:
– In “Business Process Management: Taking All the Right Steps,” Marc Hurst, Mohammad Karim
and Karthik Solaiappan offer a perspective on business process management capabilities, architecture,
development and implementation. This perspective is based on actual projects delivered to our
customers.
– In “Document Generation: Digitization in Action,” Marcia Douglas discusses a viable approach to
digitization that allows it to offer the same benefits as paper-based documents, withoutincurring the
traditional cost of paper publication.
– In “Enterprise Information Integration: Bridging the Digital Divide,” Benedicte Lambert,
Punit Kaishap and Chris Rosenberg look closely at a new technology—enterprise information
integration (EII). This article focuses on the practical aspects of this technology, including what our
customers perceive the role of an EII solution to be, as well as its benefits and challenges.
• Data For Decision delves into data and models needed for info

text:
cts of this technology, including what our
customers perceive the role of an EII solution to be, as well as its benefits and challenges.
• Data For Decision delves into data and models needed for informed decision making. When we
envision digitization, security and compliance are recurring themes that demand utmost attention.
Because this domain has grown significantly, we asked Alex Berson, who wrote “Digitization and
Security in Financial Services,” to help discuss and sort out for you the security issues associated with
digitization initiatives in a way that synthesizes what we believe the readership ought to know despite
the growing and bewildering range of issues, regulations and technology at play.
The BearingPoint professionals who contribute their insights here are eager to know how well they are
doing. We encourage you to offer feedback and ideas for future topics to Pierre Pureur, the Journal’s
editor, and our team at FSTechJournalEditors@bearingpoint.com.
Manuel Barbero
Executive Editor, BearingPoint’s Financial Services Technology Journal

4. Business process management (BPM)
tools model the processes, rules, events
and exceptions that exist throughout
all, or part of, the enterprise. By inte-
grating with workflow and the other
applications in the enterprise, BPM
helps manage the processes as they pass
between the manual and automated
systems.
5. Business intelligencetools analyze the
vast quantities of data that the enter-
prise collects and stores and turn it into
meaningful information that people
can use.
6. Collaborationtools expedite informa-
tion sharing and accelerate decision
making between teams and individuals.
These tools include online whiteboards,
bulletin boards, document collabora-
tion, chat and instant messaging.
7. Data services are a key, and often
overlooked, part of a service-orientated
architecture. They provide a way to
centralize the rules governing access to

PORTAL TECHNOLOGIES
ENTERPRISE INFORMATION INTEGRATION

1. Portal technologiesprovide a unified
work space through which users can
access the applications that are
deployed throughout the enterprise.
The portal can be used as a thin layer
on top of existing applications to create
a standardized look and feel and aid
usability. The enterprise portal also
provides the gateway through which
the other tools in the digitization stack
can be accessed.
2. Digital content management (DCM)
manages and controls unstructured
data, such as video, audio, images,
scanned documents and electronic
documents. DCM tools provide the
ability to create indices and metadata
descriptions supporting the search, as
TECHNOLOGIES ENABLING DIGITALIZATION
well as analysis and easy retrieval of
digital content.
3. Workflow handles the routing of
work between resources, whether
those resources are people, systems or
machines. It also manages the order in
which those steps are handled. At each
stage in the workflow, one individual
or group is responsible for a specific
task. Once the task is complete, the

• Originators can see the status of deals they have submitted for review.

text:
f credits in progress:
• Originators can see the status of deals they have submitted for review.
• Credit risk can see what deals are waiting on their approval and what deals are coming down the pipeline and
can research deal decisions in the past.
• Loan administrators receive all information needed to book the deal and can also see what deals are almost ready
to be booked to be able to proactively plan for periods of high and low activity.
2. Decreased cycle time—Reuse of credit package components on a future deal is now possible. New offerings need not start from
scratch, and components of earlier deals are retrievable in a standardized, repeatable fashion.
3. Full exposure understood—System shows all current credit facilities for obligor and all related obligors, allowing current credit
decisions to be made with knowledge of full exposure to this customer relationship.
4. Auto-generated documents—Documents are automatically generated by the system based on data entry. Eliminates duplicative
data entry and allows company-standard text to be used with data points filled in dynamically as needed.
5. Electronic credit file—All documents related to a credit offering, whether generat

chunk_id: /Users/manuelbarbero/Developer/AIStudio/data/corpora/demo/uploads/FS Journal, Edited by M. Barbero - Digitization.pdf::page-14::chunk-48
doc_id: /Users/manuelbarbero/Developer/AIStudio/data/corpora/demo/uploads/FS Journal, Edited by M. Barbero - Digitization.pdf
source_path: /Users/manuelbarbero/Developer/AIStudio/data/corpora/demo/uploads/FS Journal, Edited by M. Barbero - Digitization.pdf
text:
plicative
data entry and allows company-standard text to be used with data points filled in dynamically as needed.
5. Electronic credit file—All documents related to a credit offering, whether generated automatically by the system or manually
uploaded, are stored together with the offering for easy viewing and retrieval.
6. Auto-validation of credit offering—System validations determine if all required fields have been filled in, all required documents
have been uploaded, etc., and offering is rejected if incomplete. Auto-validation ensures that once credit management gets the deal,
they will have all the information necessary to make a decision.
7. Electronic routing and approval—Credit approval matrix is built into application that can route offering to appropriate approver(s)
based on deal parameters such as line of business, deal size, facility type, region and industry, among others.
8. Back-office integration—Facility type or other deal parameters are used to automatically generate booking forms for appropriate
back-office processing group(s). Back-office has all the deal terms necessary and can book the deal, or an automatic booking routine
can be written for even further ef

9. Deal archiving—All data points and documents (even multiple versions of the same document, if desired) are retained and accessible
for use in future deals or for satisfying audit and regulatory requirements.

text:
SO, ONE MORE TIME1 :
HOW DO YOU MODERNIZE LEGACY AND
(PERHAPS) MOVE TO THE CLOUD?
A PRINCIPLE-BASED APPROACH
Manuel Barbero
mbarberony@yahoo.com
+1 (917) 216-0782
1 With apologies to Herzberg [1].

So, One More Time: How Do You Modernize Legacy and (Perhaps) Move to The Cloud?
Draft 4.0 - P age 2
THE CHALLENGE
These days, boards, executive management and industry analysts demand that CTOs, CIOs and Chief
Architects visualize and implement solutions to key questions: "How are we going stop spending so
much effort and money in the very technology that keeps us stuck where you are?" and this is usually
coupled with "And why aren't we moving faster to the cloud, Isn't that the way out?"2
By now, porting to the cloud (at the application and infrastructure levels) as a modernization strategy
is "known science" with a range of patterns well-articulated starting at least about a decade ago (see
[2] and diagram, right) making generous (over-) use of the "X as a Service" trope. Software legacy
modernization has sprung an even older set of approaches. One of the most popular is the model
promoted by the Software Engineering Institute (the SEI "Horseshoe" model diagram below)

chunk_id: /Users/manuelbarbero/Developer/AIStudio/data/corpora/demo/uploads/Barbero - 2020 - Technology Modernization and Cloud Migration - 2020-02-11.pdf::page-1::chunk-1
doc_id: /Users/manuelbarbero/Developer/AIStudio/data/corpora/demo/uploads/Barbero - 2020 - Technology Modernization and Cloud Migration - 2020-02-11.pdf
source_path: /Users/manuelbarbero/Developer/AIStudio/data/corpora/demo/uploads/Barbero - 2020 - Technology Modernization and Cloud Migration - 2020-02-11.pdf
text:
Software legacy
modernization has sprung an even older set of approaches. One of the most popular is the model
promoted by the Software Engineering Institute (the SEI "Horseshoe" model diagram below) which
goes back to … 1999 [3].
This model stipulates that renovation of a given piece of software consists of two fundamental phases:
o in the Analysis phase, the
code’s “as is" structure/architecture
is recovered and is checked for conformance with
known or desired best practices/principles. The
code is first parsed (from the bottom up) for
structure patterns (essentially functions in the 1999
model) to recover the “base” structure/architecture;
o the Synthesis phase consists of applying
(hopefully better) "to be" (desired) principles to
modify the architecture at the conceptual level
which then translates into the implementation at
the logical (functions) and then code / physical
levels using an "Architecture-Driven" approach to
code development3.
One goal is to identify what are the potential
application architecture issues, such as remote call
in loops which are typically not a problem on the
mainframe and ensure that they do not become
problematic in a distributed or hybrid cloud

chunk_id: /Users/manuelbarbero/Developer/AIStudio/data/corpora/demo/uploads/Barbero - 2020 - Technology Modernization and Cloud Migration - 2020-02-11.pdf::page-1::chunk-2
doc_id: /Users/manuelbarbero/Developer/AIStudio/data/corpora/demo/uploads/Barbero - 2020 - Technology Modernization and Cloud Migration - 2020-02-11.pdf
source_path: /Users/manuelbarbero/Developer/AIStudio/data/corpora/demo/uploads/Barbero - 2020 - Technology Modernization and Cloud Migration - 2020-02-11.pdf
text:
tential
application architecture issues, such as remote call
in loops which are typically not a problem on the
mainframe and ensure that they do not become
problematic in a distributed or hybrid cloud/on premises environments when latency translates into unacceptable
performance4. This is tightly related to managing code quality standard as a whole5 - that type of remediation can be
substantially automated or systematized as we will demonstrate below.
The approach is fundamentally necessary to break monolithic applications (what is referred to as Problem A in
Attachment I – The two challenges in refactoring the application portfolio), i.e. refactoring code into components
that can decoupled and made into manageable pieces (e.g. role-based entitlement services) or into even bigger
elements ("utilities") that can be shared across applications (e.g. a centralized payment “factory” used by all
applications to interface to the rest of the world).
2 To be sure these not only some questions tormenting CIOs and CTOs. There is also the perennial "How can I be secure…?", the dreaded
"What are we doing with data?" as well as the whole spectrum of "How can I be compliant with <Insert acronyms

So, One More Time: How Do You Modernize Legacy and (Perhaps) Move to The Cloud?
Draft 4.0 - P age 3
Because it primarily focused on individual application, the narrative put together by SEI in 1999 did not put much
emphasis on how to optimize the portfolio of applications as a whole. Systematizing how applications can be
orchestrated along the line of business process/architecture (while rethinking the enterprise data and object model
across applications) is what we call Problem B in Attachment I which provides examples of both types.
Overall, the approach needs an update to show how enterprise need to literarily visualize the overall topology of the
application structure at various level of

Overall, the approach needs an update to show how enterprise need to literarily visualize the overall topology of the
application structure at various level of granularity, from code, to data and processes6. The challenge is therefore to
find a way to solve both Problem A and Problem B – neither one of them being trivial – simultaneously in an
environment where definitive answers and data is scarce and pressure to act fast is constant (See the discussion in
the Attachment II – What Is Missing From Traditional Models And Why Is It So Hard?).
A PRINCIPLE-BASED GENERAL APPROACH TO CUTTING THROUGH THE GORDIAN KNOT
Solving simultaneous problems under uncertain constraints lends itself to using a simple but not simplistic set of
known heuristics – essentially adhering to the deceivingly obvious PRINCIPLES7:
I. Know what you are solving for – Set the goal and resist deviating from it by engaging in distractions;
II. Base decisions on the accurate description of reality and think Horizontally and Vertically – Go
"wide" when analyzing the breath of issues and "deep" when planning and executing;
III. Experiment, Learn and Loop – This is the heuristic o

ption of reality and think Horizontally and Vertically – Go
"wide" when analyzing the breath of issues and "deep" when planning and executing;
III. Experiment, Learn and Loop – This is the heuristic of choice for messy/complex problems when data is
scarce;
IV. Make the Approach Sustainable – Use Machine Thinking, as defined below.
The next section provides a step-by-step implementation of a recommended approach leveraging these PRINCIPLES 8:
• PRINCIPLE I - Know what you are solving for, set the goal and resist engaging in low-value distractions.
Knowing what you should be solving for is not obvious9.
"Going to the cloud", "Implement a microservice architecture" should not be considered ultimate goals in-and-
out themselves - merely possible approaches to serve a higher purpose10. This said, even when non-
technologists cannot articulate what a good technology strategy is, they can often “tell when they see one". So,
as many companies tout themselves as de facto technology firms, it is not unfair for CIOs/CTOs to be asked to
propose their goals and to demonstrate how they can be traced to their understanding of the business needs11.

So, One More Time: How Do You Modernize Legacy and (Perhaps) Move to The Cloud?
Draft 4.0 - P age 4
So, what IS a good goal? Here are two simple rules/tests:
A worthwhile goal should be articulated in a way that the business can value and at a level that the business
can relate to. "Create customer loyalty and thus recurring revenues by providing a seamless, superior user
experience across devices and entry points into the firm" is one example of tangible objective at the highest
level (Level 1). Achieving that goal might requires solving issues at a lower level (Level 2) e.g. "renormalizing
data models”, “implementing rule-based workflow management" or "moving from batch to event-driven
architectures". This, in turn, might require achieving goals at the even lower level (Level 3) through a
combination of techn

text:
ting rule-based workflow management" or "moving from batch to event-driven
architectures". This, in turn, might require achieving goals at the even lower level (Level 3) through a
combination of technology-specific approaches or paradigms (say, using the micro-services or object-
orientation architecture styles). But starting at level 1 is a prerequisite as jumping to other levels is misguided if
these subaltern goals can’t be traced to a Level 1 goal. They are at any rate, meaningless to a business audience.
Another useful way at testing if a goal at any level is indeed worthy of attention is to ensure that it will
position the firm to generally adapt and move fast as business conditions change12. In effect, many
investments in technology, (particularly infrastructure) should be priced as barrier options, a way to acquire the
ability, in the future, to makes yet-undefined but demonstrably valuable activities possible [9]. Indeed, this is
fundamentally why legacy technology is an issue: it's a burden that needs to be carried forward and slows the
enterprise down by reducing its ability to adapt. In the previous paragraph, the goal of introducing rule-based,
event-driven workflows i

keep the user experience seamless and rewarding.
• PRINCIPLE IIA – Base decisions on the accurate description of reality – Know when to go wide to analyze
the breath of issues – Once the goal is set (i.e. what do we fundamentally need to do to be ready to adapt),
knowing what to tackle and in which order is critical.
A faux-truism13 is that planning should be fact-based14. It demands commitment to radical transparency and
open-mindedness to systematically looking at instances when things went wrong against a well-defined goal,
then specifically identifying what prevents you from achieving the goal, before looking at the various possible
options15 and opting for the optimal one, i.e. the one most likely to address the root cause(s) of the failures16.
The quest for understanding what you have to deal with at the aggregate and what to do about it is probably the
single biggest issue facing technologists

The quest for understanding what you have to deal with at the aggregate and what to do about it is probably the
single biggest issue facing technologists when they attempt to scope out their plan and this is a step currently
missing from the SEI Horseshoe model.
From a practical standpoint that means making sure that you pick and choose which application to target first
(and why) based on a fact-based analysis. It also means setting up the right metrics to track how your trajectory
stays within a valid cone of expectation17. Both require the analytical framework presented next.

roadmaps, and ensuring that the right remedial actions are taken when implementation of these plan are not going as planned, this are
merely activities necessary to make informed decisions based on sound principles and Truth (as defined in footnote 14).

in fact is much more insightful that it appears and quite difficult, in fact, to adhere to. Many (if not all) Principles qualify as faux-truisms.

In fact, navigating the levels of code coarseness is critical to form a high level and then increasingly refined
view of what needs to be dealt with23.
The diagram below24 shows an example of such a view. In this simple case, the architect has identified a component
of the architecture (use of obsolete STRUTS framework in the upper left corner) as a candidate for replacement.

• PRINCIPLE III - Experiment, Learn and Loop - That solving complex problems (such as organizing the
rewrite of a portfolio of legacy apps) should be an iterative process is now a trope widely understood by
technologists. It is for example, at its core a foundational element of agile thinking.
Yet without incremental leverage of lessons learned, looping is merely churning. Many organizations tackle
legacy application remediation as individual projects. One of the reasons is lack or cross-portfolio governance
(see next PRINCIPLE) or just the operational difficulty of having several teams making time to harvest, much less
share, lessons learned in their individual attempts to remedy codes.
But at its core, this missed opportunity stems from the simple fact that organizations – in an attempt to "stay
focused" – do not recognize harvesting and leveraging lessons learned across projects as a worthwhile goal.
This is something that keeps organizations on a low growth trajectory. Sure, the "retrospective" step in agile
development is an instantiation of such thinking a

This is something that keeps organizations on a low growth trajectory. Sure, the "retrospective" step in agile
development is an instantiation of such thinking at play but in practice, the systematically harvesting and
sharing knowledge gained from refactoring is rarely promoted across the organization25.
Minimally, an understanding of how each application models data and business objects should be part of what
each team should document so that the overall enterprise data ontology can be shaped, and data representation
be rationalized in, for example, well structure data domains. This is why this step is explicitly introduced in the
recommended new approach (next section).

So, One More Time: How Do You Modernize Legacy and (Perhaps) Move to The Cloud?
Draft 4.0 - P age 7
• PRINCIPLE IV - Make it sustainable: Machine Thinking - When it comes to application modernization (or
remediation) many technology organizations fail to set-up processes that can be used over and over. This is a
failure to apply Machine Thinking.
Machine Thinking26 is a way to operate well and deliver good results by insuring that
o a goal is well defined to deliver a specific outcome;
o a person (Responsible Party or RP) is responsible to achieve it;
o the adequate group of people are selected to operate a series of well-defined processes (collectively,
the machine’s design); and crucially,
o the RP is under the obligation to constantly look at the machine's outcome and tweak the overall
machine's design (people, processes) to improve the trajectory of

chunk_id: /Users/manuelbarbero/Developer/AIStudio/data/corpora/demo/uploads/Barbero - 2020 - Technology Modernization and Cloud Migration - 2020-02-11.pdf::page-7::chunk-25

text:
e machine’s design); and crucially,
o the RP is under the obligation to constantly look at the machine's outcome and tweak the overall
machine's design (people, processes) to improve the trajectory of execution vis-a-vis the desired
outcome.
Machine Thinking seems obvious, but this is another faux-truism: the reader might ask themselves how many
times one or more of the characteristics above are missing from their engagements with others. The reality is
that unclear goals, dilution of responsibilities, inadequate staffing (size and abilities/skills) and half-backed or
undefined processes are rampant in most organizations. We also noted above how rarely organizations
implement or value the need to develop systematic, continuous improvement27.
SO, ONE MORE TIME: HOW DO YOU MODERNIZE LEGACY AND (PERHAPS) MOVE TO THE CLOUD? A STEP-BY-
STEP APPROACH
Machine Thinking requires that we close the loop on the SEI horseshoe model (and probably find a new name – we
will call it the Modified SEI (Horseshoe) Model).
After the all-important steps of setting both expectations (Step 0) and the support infrastructure (Step 1 - Tools,
people and process) and a first cut at prioritizing the portfolio

After the all-important steps of setting both expectations (Step 0) and the support infrastructure (Step 1 - Tools,
people and process) and a first cut at prioritizing the portfolio (Step 2), the approach consists in a series of loops
(Step 3) that builds on the SEI model.
Step 0 – Set Expectations
• Communicate the goal and approach with stakeholders and get in hard sync with them28 – The
expectations should be that this will be a journey. And that the journey will have both an execution dimension
(where results will be achieved) as well as an – intermingled – on-going learning and refinement dimension (to
constantly improve a Machinery).
• Size-up the opportunity at the high level – Compile a broad outline of what can be said about the overall size
of the effort and therefore its duration (using T-shirt sizes for both and ideally, Monte Carlo analysis to define
confidence ranges [7]). But be ready to push back on demand for a traditional "plan". This is a case when, by
design, the plan consists in setting a direction and looping through an iterative process which itself will evolve
over time.

and gathering high level data) will be modest, leading to an informed go/no go decision before proceeding
further.

Step 3 – Run the steps of a modified version of the SEI model presented earlier in this article.
There are essentially four core modifications to the model (see diagram below):
A – Have each loop start with a reassessment of priorities along key dimensions (PRINCIPLE I and IIA), refining
what was done on Step 2 at each round;
B – Put emphasis on identifying not just execution logic but also harvest business logic and data structure
information (ideally in an object-oriented model) (PRINCIPLE IIB);
C – Constantly look for optimizing the portfolio by:
• Refactoring the applications in modules that can be shared across applications; and,
• Implementing cross application orchestration to support better architected business processes.
This is where we can leverage the significant body of work that has been developed by industry groups for best
practices in:
• Performance/Efficiency [11],
• Security [12];
• Code quality in general37

. Starting with a Proof of
Concept of the approach and moving on to Pilots for a small group of application specifically selected for their
potential is the way to start.
• Q: How do we know we are targeting the right goals and that we are on the right trajectory to get there?
A: The approach calls for a periodic and explicit measurement of achievements against fact-based information.
This can be done by scanning the portfolio both in depth and in breath to guide priorities. Selecting which
applications should be modernized (and/or ported to the cloud) will be based on known rational criteria that
will also be used to track the trajectory of the overall project. All this is can and will be aided by tools which
makes it realistic to manage the trajectory of such projects in a systematic, data-driven way.
• Q: When will this be done? How much will it cost?
A: Those are questions that should only answered at the by-and-large level first and refined over time. The
alternative is to commit to a flawed and unrealistic plan. Initial investment should be modest at first because a
big-bang approach usually fails.
Our hope is that the discussion will not only guide practitioners in achieving

to a flawed and unrealistic plan. Initial investment should be modest at first because a
big-bang approach usually fails.
Our hope is that the discussion will not only guide practitioners in achieving their goal but also disseminate
common-sense ideas that executive management can attach to regardless of their technology proficiency.
And what common sense has taught us is that in an environment where definitive answers and data is scarce and
pressure to act fast is relentless, setting up relevant goals and set fact-based expectations is key. That implies
avoiding jumping to quick conclusions and constantly adjusting estimates and approach along the way by setting up
the overall project as an evolving machinery.
In addition, nowadays and fortunately, tools that can really help remove the tediousness of gathering and
synthesizing key information about the enterprise software (both in terms of breath of portfolio and dependencies)
and deep into the code up all the way to the overall ecosystem architecture.

CASE STUDY – CLOUD MIGRATION – IF YOU DO ONLY ONE THING PRIOR TO SHIFT AND LIFT
Consider the following application (illustrated at left), typical of today’s
architecture:
• A front-end made of HTML5/JavaScript/modern frameworks
accessing a back-end of services through REST calls composed
of two additional layers;
• A business logic layer developed for example in Java;
• The data access layer in Java tool leveraging a persistence
framework and the data services proper, for example, a regular
RDBMS and a NoSQL platform for the unstructured data.
In a classic on-site implementation, all the components would be running
on the same network using a properly dimensioned compute and storage
infrastructure built with this assumption in mind (i.e. accesses to data is
fast and cheap).
In this environment, is not uncommon that the communication between the layers, specifically from the business
logic to the data storage layer to not be optimized and/or reduced to the minimum. Developers may have even
l

chunk_id: /Users/manuelbarbero/Developer/AIStudio/data/corpora/demo/uploads/Barbero - 2020 - Technology Modernization and Cloud Migration - 2020-02-11.pdf::page-13::chunk-44
doc_id: /Users/manuelbarbero/Developer/AIStudio/data/corpora/demo/uploads/Barbero - 2020 - Technology Modernization and Cloud Migration - 2020-02-11.pdf
source_path: /Users/manuelbarbero/Developer/AIStudio/data/corpora/demo/uploads/Barbero - 2020 - Technology Modernization and Cloud Migration - 2020-02-11.pdf
text:
is not uncommon that the communication between the layers, specifically from the business
logic to the data storage layer to not be optimized and/or reduced to the minimum. Developers may have even
leveraged the opportunity of fast data accesses and/but then (alas) adopt non optimized patterns such as looping calls
from the business logic layers to the data storage layers instead of writing a single optimized query to gather all the
data in one call.
Once moved on a cloud infrastructure, the assumption at the core of the architectural and development choices will
no longer be valid anymore as one has little control on how the different services will be deployed by the cloud
service provider. For example, by default, the performances of the exchanges between the different virtual machines
is constrained by a range of implementation consideration including cost.
One remedy would be to implement a dedicated set of machines with a significant guaranteed bandwidth in between
the machines, reproducing the configuration that was on premises, but that would defeat the original intent of
moving to the cloud (cost reduction, dynamic resource allocation…).
The situation can be made worse if,

chunk_id: /Users/manuelbarbero/Developer/AIStudio/data/corpora/demo/uploads/Barbero - 2020 - Technology Modernization and Cloud Migration - 2020-02-11.pdf::page-13::chunk-45
doc_id: /Users/manuelbarbero/Developer/AIStudio/data/corpora/demo/uploads/Barbero - 2020 - Technology Modernization and Cloud Migration - 2020-02-11.pdf
source_path: /Users/manuelbarbero/Developer/AIStudio/data/corpora/demo/uploads/Barbero - 2020 - Technology Modernization and Cloud Migration - 2020-02-11.pdf
text:
reproducing the configuration that was on premises, but that would defeat the original intent of
moving to the cloud (cost reduction, dynamic resource allocation…).
The situation can be made worse if, to preserve data confidentiality or to satisfy other compliance constraints, the
data is persisted on premise. Then the hundreds of exchanges between the business logic and the data storage layer
that were easily absorbed by the on-premise infrastructure become a bottleneck and eventually impacts
performances. What makes this insidious is that these issues can crop-out over time unnoticed at first but quite
perplexing over time.
And even in the absence of loops in the design and despite the use of persistence frameworks, things can still go
wrong: it took once a team of subcontractors about a week to identify that caching parameters were wrongly set in
the Hibernate framework they used – This goes to show that systematic review of code delivered by third parties
should go beyond just detecting conformance to security-related coding practices. And that regardless of how good
your teams are, you can't assume that your vendor implementation team is intimately aware of the innards of the

chunk_id: /Users/manuelbarbero/Developer/AIStudio/data/corpora/demo/uploads/Barbero - 2020 - Technology Modernization and Cloud Migration - 2020-02-11.pdf::page-14::chunk-46
doc_id: /Users/manuelbarbero/Developer/AIStudio/data/corpora/demo/uploads/Barbero - 2020 - Technology Modernization and Cloud Migration - 2020-02-11.pdf
source_path: /Users/manuelbarbero/Developer/AIStudio/data/corpora/demo/uploads/Barbero - 2020 - Technology Modernization and Cloud Migration - 2020-02-11.pdf
text:
cting conformance to security-related coding practices. And that regardless of how good
your teams are, you can't assume that your vendor implementation team is intimately aware of the innards of the
solution they help deploy.
The smart move here is to identify all potential issues in the application before the actual move. There are various
approaches to eradicate the issue. That includes re-performing unit test to profile where bottlenecks are. Better yet,
use automated code analysis technology described in this article to spot engineering anti-patterns across multi-tier
architecture, one that can also scan through persistence frameworks38.
38 See footnote 6 about https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Software_modernization

APPENDIX I - THE TWO CHALLENGES IN REFACTORING THE APPLICATION PORTFOLIO
Organizations place an intense focus on individual legacy application modernization (we will call this Problem A.
Take, for instance, a commission system used by financial services organizations to manage the variety of
compensation schemes used to incentivize their distribution channel

chunk_id: /Users/manuelbarbero/Developer/AIStudio/data/corpora/demo/uploads/Barbero - 2020 - Technology Modernization and Cloud Migration - 2020-02-11.pdf::page-14::chunk-47
doc_id: /Users/manuelbarbero/Developer/AIStudio/data/corpora/demo/uploads/Barbero - 2020 - Technology Modernization and Cloud Migration - 2020-02-11.pdf
source_path: /Users/manuelbarbero/Developer/AIStudio/data/corpora/demo/uploads/Barbero - 2020 - Technology Modernization and Cloud Migration - 2020-02-11.pdf
text:
will call this Problem A.
Take, for instance, a commission system used by financial services organizations to manage the variety of
compensation schemes used to incentivize their distribution channels (independent or captive agents).
For example, service S sold by agent A will result in a one-time commission C(P,A), a function of S and A. But
things can be vastly more complex, for example in the life insurance industry, a Policy P was sold n years ago under
discount structure D by an agent will translate into a recurrent commission C(P,A,n,D) to A. And the rules become
even more complex for team sales and other promotions schemes introduced over time.
Ideally, these systems should consist in:
• A data ingestion and normalization layer that captures the relevant information required to compute
rewards, ideally from various systems of record for a) service-specific characteristics / pricing and b)
distribution agent- specific attributes;
• A rule engine that applies the necessary algorithms like the formula above;
A synthetization layer that aggreges results at the agent level (to be published say, every month);
• A general cross-system workflow and exception management sub-system.

text:
thms like the formula above;
A synthetization layer that aggreges results at the agent level (to be published say, every month);
• A general cross-system workflow and exception management sub-system.
In practices, legacy systems evolve in such a way that these layers are seldom so neatly decoupled or even logically
organized39. In addition, rules are hard-coded in a series of lengthy "if..then,.. clauses" that are very risky to change
over time. The whole thing becomes very brittle40 and soon, no new product can be introduced, endangering the
long-term relevance of the company. This is a typical Problem A.
But even if these monolithic systems are re-factored, one need to give a lot of attention to how to re-stich together
these applications in a way that create velocity and economies of scale and scope at the enterprise level.
If one merely decouples the components of monolithic apps without recoupling refactored components into re-
usable, components that can be mutualized across the firm, one fails to leverage an opportunity to truly digitize the
organization at the business process level.
This is not just a “nice to have” – It is difficult if not impossible to create integrated

firm, one fails to leverage an opportunity to truly digitize the
organization at the business process level.
This is not just a “nice to have” – It is difficult if not impossible to create integrated business processes if they are
impeded by the fragmentation of the underlying application portfolio. That translates into disjointed user
experiences (for example having to re-enter data at the home office or in the field when jumping from one
application-specific screen to another).
And it is also a lost opportunity to redesign so-called User Journey(s) (or the home office workers' daily experience)
and make them smooth across platforms at each contact point ("Moment of Truth"). In fact, not rethinking how the
data flows and the underlying data models can be recomposed is not just a huge missed opportunity but a guarantee
that the actual fragmentation of user experience will continue. It makes a key worthwhile goal (as defined in the
what is a good goal? discussion in this paper) impossible.
This is Problem B: how to modernize the application and integrate then to implement actual intelligent, integrated
workflows and introduce economies of scale and scope. The only way to achieve a w

This is Problem B: how to modernize the application and integrate then to implement actual intelligent, integrated
workflows and introduce economies of scale and scope. The only way to achieve a worthy goal like Creating
customer loyalty and thus recurring revenues by providing a seamless, superior user experience across devices and
entry points into the firm cited earlier in this article.

it, if for example one relies on batch to process large amount of data when storage costs were at a premium, probably not the best under

For example, take an organization which already manages a multitude of CRM systems, say Microsoft Dynamics
which might have been painfully deployed in the field over a lengthy period of time to replace a legacy Siebel
system. Meanwhile Marketing might have been dabbling with Salesforce to support their campaigns. A previous
version of Salesforce might have been deployed by the Call Centers to manage user requests.
And to top it off, none of them might be using the same access controls or one developed elsewhere in the
organization.
Familiar?
Predictably, in this situation, voice will raise to consolidate it all in one single system.
Yet doing so is unlikely to yield significant benefits, at least not for a long time.
But this is actually an opportunity.
In this case, the right solution might be to rethink how each platform activities can be coupled through an
orchestration and data sharing layer while a common role-based access control system is implemented.
But to be clear, implementing such an approach is tricky too: some organizations try to create a full-fledged
(enterprise) busses and enterprise-wide orchestration engines to connect all possible a

chunk_id: /Users/manuelbarbero/Developer/AIStudio/data/corpora/demo/uploads/Barbero - 2020 - Technology Modernization and Cloud Migration - 2020-02-11.pdf::page-15::chunk-52

d.
But to be clear, implementing such an approach is tricky too: some organizations try to create a full-fledged
(enterprise) busses and enterprise-wide orchestration engines to connect all possible applications. Some create vast
shared data repositories. Like promoting an all-API architecture to glue the ecosystem of applications, each of these
efforts tend to collapse on their own weight if they are considered as a goal in-and-out themselves41.
Given all the above and without getting into more details, solving problem A and B seems to be best done by:
• Introducing an explicit orchestrating interaction layer across apps (or their newly refactored versions);
• Using event-driven architecture, implement the right patterns to exchange data across apps such as the
lambda architecture or data streaming data architectures, generally, good Level 2 goals;
• Careful using processes-aligned, rule-based engines, much more flexible than imbedded code-base rules.
This approach also has the advantage of lending itself to incremental implementation through the Proof of Concept /
Pilot / Scaling paradigm promoted in the Modified SEI Model.
It also helps rethinking the enterprise data model and i

age of lending itself to incremental implementation through the Proof of Concept /
Pilot / Scaling paradigm promoted in the Modified SEI Model.
It also helps rethinking the enterprise data model and identify the minimal necessary subset of data that can and
should be mutualized across applications in a realist, incremental way.
41 It is true that the existence of Problem B is related to the persistence (for example) of batch processes as a somewhat primitive approach to
implement orchestration. But substituting batch by having apps communicate through APIs merely address some aspects of the problem. It
indeed improves real time performance and efficiency at the development level (code reusability, decoupling of technology components).
Yet, the stateless nature of the API paradigm does not really help creating true, rule-based orchestration across applications. Performance is
also an issue as APIs calls must pass context data, which introduces latency (in addition to the type of aforementioned spaghetti architecture.

APPENDIX II – WHAT IS MISSING FROM THE TRADITIONAL MODELS AND WHY IS IT SO HARD?
"As is", the Gartner and the SEI model/methodologies defined at the onset of this article are helpful in proposing
execution choices and approaches but there are other, residual, practical questions facing CTOs and ICOs:
• How do we get started?
• How do we know we are targeting the right goal and that we are on the right trajectory to get there?
• When will this be done? How much will it cost?
Fundamentally, the challenge is the dearth of actionable information. This makes the questions above challenging to
settle conclusively and creates a lot of unnecessary churn:
• At the highest level, many technology practitioners spend massive amount of time with limited success in
trying to answer questions like what are our critical apps?, what is the marginal cost of maintaining each
app? or even, at a higher level what are the business priorities and how do I map them into my technology
agenda? – all in an attempt to apply a rigorous approach to decision making. The

text:
taining each
app? or even, at a higher level what are the business priorities and how do I map them into my technology
agenda? – all in an attempt to apply a rigorous approach to decision making. The reality of that trying to
answer these questions too precisely is, as discussed in this article, probably impossible to do but,
thankfully, not necessary;
• In the technology realm, many organizations attempt to refactor applications – all at once. The issue is even
more complex when the teams attempt to rationalize each application data model along the way. Again,
probably not a realistic goal but in fact not a good goal because it leads to risk analysis paralysis;
• Generally, putting a price tag on the overall endeavor even for the 1 to 3 year horizon can be an exercise in
futility (although many vendors will be happy to perform analysis in a few weeks – usually only on a subset
of apps – the ones they know or already operate on an outsourcing basis) leading to many high-precision,
low accuracy calculations.
Also, if you think this is hard, you are not alone. Here are the sobering numbers, collected from several sources:
there are now (2019) about half a billion worldwide applicatio

accuracy calculations.
Also, if you think this is hard, you are not alone. Here are the sobering numbers, collected from several sources:
there are now (2019) about half a billion worldwide applications slated for modernization42 with Lift and Shift being
the prominent approach but with a success rate less than 40%43. The same data source states that the transformation
journey will take more than a year and 43% more than two years44. In that study, 62% of people interviewed said
that the projects were harder than expected or that they failed, and 55% said that they went over budget. And it's not
surprising:
• Building new apps using a new modern stack (on the cloud or on-promises but with internet-derived
technologies (e.g. containers, service-oriented architectures) and practices (e.g. DevOps)) will not obviate
the need to accept the legacy technology as a massive boundary condition. One needs to accept that the
overall technology landscape cannot be truly rationalized. For example, data residing on the legacy
platforms will have to be sourced as is and not rethought in the context on a better, rationalized cross-
application enterprise data architecture and governance model.
• Wh

ata residing on the legacy
platforms will have to be sourced as is and not rethought in the context on a better, rationalized cross-
application enterprise data architecture and governance model.
• When pushed to go to the cloud, technologists try to check the box by doing a lot of Lift and Shift,
something that rarely cuts cost (compute and storage unit costs are NOT always competitive on the cloud
when compared to those achieved on premises by large organization). Lift and Shift certainly does NOT
provide new business capabilities. In addition, it not only creates new transition costs but also opportunity
costs by monopolizing the few key people knowledgeable about legacy systems whose insight could be
leveraged (while they are still around) to create better solutions.
There's got to be a better way, one which, as proposed in this article, is principled-based and supports a portfolio-
wide yet iterative road to success.

Key message #3 - leverage known best practices (research and review in progress)
Big Data Best Practices from Industry
■ Any data from any source can be acquired, ingested, and processed
■ All data are uniquely identified, time-tagged, and classified at the time of ingestion for
control and absolute traceability in space and time
■ Pre-position data for performance-data is dist

chunk_id: /Users/manuelbarbero/Developer/AIStudio/data/corpora/demo/uploads/Data Strategy - 2021-01-08a.pdf::page-10::chunk-10

text:
cessed
■ All data are uniquely identified, time-tagged, and classified at the time of ingestion for
control and absolute traceability in space and time
■ Pre-position data for performance-data is distributed into multiple engines for consumption
and analysis and pre-indexed for known patterns, use models, and correlations
■ Data is stored in multiple repositories and in multiple forms to meet business needs
■ Design for real-time--maximize use of in-memory processing; keep data close to compute;
move the smallest objects
■ Multi-format data discovery- ability to navigate, search, and discover data independent of
the formats
■ Latency breeds contempt - data is always available for consumption in a form that does not
require upfront conditioning
■ Enable learning feedback to create, preserve and apply knowledge and learning through the
system
■ Continuous refactoring based on cumulative learning and knowledge -data is constantly
reconditioned and re-correlated as knowledge evolves and learning occurs

Key message #4 - use principled thinking
DaaS architecture high-level principles
■ Do No Harm-Ensure that ongoing data and business activities are not adversely affected while building and standing­up the Data
as a Service fabric. Leverage ongoing best practices where appropriate.
■ Build for Security - Strong security controls are built in at the onset and designed to include monitoring functions and enable easy
sockets integration to assure complete situational awareness.
■ Cloud First-Leverage for cloud-based, Infrastructure and Platform as a Service capabilities consistent with the TD goals and
objectives.
■ Loosely Coupled Systems - A well-documented specification for communications and data interchanges between system
components that facilitates the easy addition of new technologies into the system to address new business use cases.
■ Continuous Availability - Design and build to ensure systems are always available by leveraging multi-zone, multi-cloud distribution
of data, platforms, and services.
■ Everything in Real Time - Design and build systems to maximize speed and performance, minimize latency, and operate in real-time
at petabyte and beyond

chunk_id: /Users/manuelbarbero/Developer/AIStudio/data/corpora/demo/uploads/Data Strategy - 2021-01-08a.pdf::page-10::chunk-12

text:
distribution
of data, platforms, and services.
■ Everything in Real Time - Design and build systems to maximize speed and performance, minimize latency, and operate in real-time
at petabyte and beyond scales.
■ Scalability-Implement dynamically scalable data storage and processing capabilities to sustain extreme data processing and
transactional loads for complex event processing and real time analytics.
■ Agile Data Modeling-Apply an iterative, incremental, and evolutionary approach to data modeling. Adopt best big data practices of
late binding and schema on write. Implement flexible and extensible data models to support migrations, upgrades, and refactoring of
data.
■ Learning Feedback-Enable learning to be fed back into the system to build, enhance and leverage corporate knowledge.
■ Lightweight/Lean Data Governance-Focus data governance to assure the availability, integrity, security, usability, and quality of
data.
■ Standardization - Ensure enterprise-wide standards for investment management technology and process solutions where possible
relying on industry standards.
■ Data Integrity - Apply business owned, automated procedures to ensure consistency and quality of data acr

text:
because the

typical IT organization is still hindered by a number of legacy decisions leading to sub-
optimal design:
• A number of organizations, particularly in the financial services industry, are the
product of many mergers. After a merger, and this is true for IT and every other
function, certain principles are invoked, ranging from the “let’s leverage leading
practices from both camps” approach to “let’s repaint everything in the colors of
the acquiring company’s logo.”
But often neither of these “leading-practice/merger-of-equals” or “takeover” scenarios
really happens. Instead, whole portions of the resulting merged organization are
assigned to executives of the legacy organizations through a protracted process that
protects some individual careers without too much logic, aside from the desire to
define a “politically balanced” organization.
• When various IT groups are consolidated, there is very frequently no conscious
attempt to deal with the necessary responsibility overlaps and redundancies beyond
a superficial aggregation of activities. This issue is particularly true at the executive
level and in situations involving outsourcing when organizational c

chunk_id: /Users/manuelbarbero/Developer/AIStudio/data/corpora/demo/uploads/Architecture Concepts and How To Use Them To Design an Organizations.pdf::page-4::chunk-3

text:
nsibility overlaps and redundancies beyond
a superficial aggregation of activities. This issue is particularly true at the executive
level and in situations involving outsourcing when organizational charts continue to
include functions and people that presumably need to be outsourced.
• Complex organizational governance models are built around personalities instead of
the result of a deliberate process. Typically, technologists, but also non-technologists,
are put in charge of managerial duties for which they are not necessarily prepared.
Finally, to compound the problem, solutions for these issues are often implemented
without using or invoking explicit principles or actual facts. This is particularly problematic
when dealing with an audience of technologists who tend to respect and promote systematic
process and adherence to standards and methodologies.

text:
s and Constructs
Questions such as where, how and by whom should architecture be defined can
be elegantly answered using the very principles employed to define a technology
architecture itself — a duality that is well illustrated in Figure 1, Drawing Hands by
M.C. Escher.
As a result, when attempts are made to mold the organization around existing people
and skill sets, employees impacted by the change not only begin to question the decisions
made but to resist their implementation while demanding to know how decisions were made
and how fair the process has been. This invariably leads to situations in which management
goes through a “decide and announce” or “decide and sell” approach, with neither succeeding
in rallying employees to support change.
There must be a better way.
ThInKInG LIKe an archITecT
Designing an IT organization, or any organization for that matter, should be the product
of a systematic analysis that defines the bridge between the business vision, organizational
constructs, processes and enabling technologies required to execute the vision.
The organizational architecture is the foundation upon which decisions are made,
processes are developed, and supporting tec

text:
tructs, processes and enabling technologies required to execute the vision.
The organizational architecture is the foundation upon which decisions are made,
processes are developed, and supporting technologies are selected and implemented so that
business processes are served.
So here is the challenge: How do you resolve the system of simultaneous equations that
define each component of the model and determine how they interact together? Moreover,
how do you accomplish all of this with the typical “boundary conditions” all organizations
face — for example, the lack of complete or even partial understanding of either the current
state or uncertainty as to what the end state should be. Architects have to deal with this level
of complexity and uncertainty all the time.
In the sidebar, “A Universal Architecture Toolset,” are several of the approaches regularly
leveraged in architecting technology solutions. These approaches can be used in architecting
organizations, too. Each of these approaches is essentially transposable “as is” from the IT
architectural realm to organization architecture.
We will review each of these approaches in turn with a brief description of the underlying
conc

oaches is essentially transposable “as is” from the IT
architectural realm to organization architecture.
We will review each of these approaches in turn with a brief description of the underlying
concepts, examples of use in the technology realm, and a description of how to use this
approach in the context of organizational design and transformation.
The concepts (some would say the “meta-patterns”) are presented in a logical order so
that one concept can be explained by previously introduced references but the order does not
reflect the relative degree of importance of each tool, which should be viewed as a component
of an overall toolset.

This contrasts with a perhaps less intuitive, but at times more powerful, approach in
terms of how fast one can get to results and how good the results are. In the so-called iterative
process, there is a constant attempt to retrace your steps and improve on previous results
through incremental changes. This iterative approach can be taken at various levels — for
example:
• Project level iteration. A project team might decide to develop a first release of a
solution with limited features, knowing full well that this will not be the final result.
Each successive iteration of the activities is then built on the results of the previous
one until the project is declared complete because the current result is good
enough to be accepted by the stakeholders.
• Task level iteration. The same approach can apply at the task level. For example,
a document evolves and each draft incorporates new information.

combinations of ceremony and flow is out of the scope of this paper, but a few remarks are
in order:
• High ceremony using the waterfall approach has been a common, essentially de facto,
approach in large projects, particularly those mandated by governmental agencies,
which have actually published standards, such as DOD-STD-2167A, MIL-STD-498,
where a high level of risk management and traceability of results are demanded.
• This has also been traditionally used by systems integration departments with similar

text:
f a long, mostly opaque process.
Considering the constant acceleration of business cycles, these types of approaches
have been very adaptable in the current business environment where long term
essentially means “within the current budgetary cycle.”
But there is more to agile development processes. Their success stems from their
essential pragmatism and the fact that value is delivered very early and increases at
each iteration. High ceremony and waterfall approaches assume and demand a perhaps
unrealistic type of stability in the user environment — for example, requirements not
changing for the entire duration of what are sometimes years of development — or
users’ patience in getting something in their hands at the end of a long, mostly opaque
process. At that point, trying to make change is too late.
application to IT organizational Design
Envision your first day in leading an IT transformational change focusing on organization.
Whether it is a wholesale multiyear project or the redesign of the inner workings of a single
department, you will have to decide what will work best.
For large, enterprisewide projects, there is probably no other way but to adopt a high
level of formalit

text:
sign of the inner workings of a single
department, you will have to decide what will work best.
For large, enterprisewide projects, there is probably no other way but to adopt a high
level of formality and linearity in execution. If employees are widely dispersed geographi-
cally, communication has to be done with some level of ceremony, not only because of practical
considerations — a lot of one-to-many communications are needed — but also because the
potential for ambiguity increases exponentially with the size of the project. This requires
a substantial amount of well-calibrated, scripted communication.6
For example, reorganizing the infrastructure component of an IT group — that is, the
one dealing with network and data centers — might take several years just because of the
sheer size of the project, so there is no practical alternative to executing change using a
waterfall approach.

But this is not a general rule. More and more engineering-oriented organizations have

a similar approach to organizational transformation based on incremental improvement with
a lot of one-on-one consultation, as opposed to the more formal use of “all hands” meetings —
that is, the use of less ceremony. Look for this type of change if similar approaches using

Finally, large and complex does not necessarily imply high ceremony and waterfall
(Case Study 1).
Overall, the agile-iterative approach (agile) is more suited to organizations with highly
competent and self-motivated individuals. These individuals also tend to be averse to “strict”
methodologies and the associated rigors, and they are better suited to the more “laissez-faire”
attitude encouraged by agile. To that end, organizational designs should leverage a rapidly
changing agile-based approach primarily in the setup of Engineering Centers of Excellence
(COE) — further discussed in section 4, “Using Value Chain Analysis,” where highly competent
individuals address complex architectural issues, evaluate cutting-edge technologies and
their relevance, and develop reusable components and frameworks. The work done here is
then leveraged across the enterprise.

• Typically executives need (or rather want without necessarily questioning why they
do so) to be presented with information with a high degree of formality/cere

• Employees impacted by change need to be provided with more than an abstract

process will be iterative in nature and that the results of each iteration will be
presented to executive management as a milestone.

Here is a case study that shows how an enterprise-level transformation process was dealt with successfully,
using an iterative, low ceremony approach to accelerate the use of offshore resources for application
development.
A company with a substantial amount of activity focusing on the development of ERP and customized
software solutions for third parties wanted to boost the use of offshore resources. In the past, the
use of these resources had remained at a relatively low level since the firm established a few new
development centers abroad.
The previous attempts to encourage the use of these centers have used intensive top-down encourage-
ments, including highly visible and repeated announcements of the opening of the centers and even special
inter

chunk_id: /Users/manuelbarbero/Developer/AIStudio/data/corpora/demo/uploads/Architecture Concepts and How To Use Them To Design an Organizations.pdf::page-9::chunk-20

text:
s attempts to encourage the use of these centers have used intensive top-down encourage-
ments, including highly visible and repeated announcements of the opening of the centers and even special
internal pricing to encourage development groups to leverage the new centers. Executive management
designed detailed, but ultimately unrealistic, growth plans dutifully agreed to by all but not implemented
in practice.
The use of these centers only took off after one of the business unit leaders took a very different tack.
He asked for a very small budget to create an internal technology interface group (TIG) to liaise with the
centers and to subsidize some projects so that they could use that new TIG entity. He did not present a
detailed ROI analysis. Nor did he suggest additional enterprise communication, recommending instead
that a low-key approach be used. In particular, he insisted on personally marketing the use of the new
TIG within the company. He also predicted correctly that the success of the project would be achieved
through a form of viral marketing — i.e., that the success of the initial projects would attract other
groups to use the TIG. In his own words, “We’ll make this a s

chunk_id: /Users/manuelbarbero/Developer/AIStudio/data/corpora/demo/uploads/Architecture Concepts and How To Use Them To Design an Organizations.pdf::page-10::chunk-21

text:
ss of the project would be achieved
through a form of viral marketing — i.e., that the success of the initial projects would attract other
groups to use the TIG. In his own words, “We’ll make this a skunk works group that will work through
word of mouth.”
What he called “creating the buzz” did indeed create iterative levels of success and significant growth
in the use of offshore centers where a more formal approach had previously failed.

Every problem, whether it is related to technology or organizational design,
can and should be considered along a number of relevant dimensions.
summary of Key Ideas
• Understand the high and low ceremony and iterative/waterfall concepts and
determine early on what will work best for your initiative.
• Do not assume that technologists only favor one approach over the other. Instead,
assume they understand these concepts because they have to deal with them in the
context of the execution of technology projects.
• Define and use this terminology with team members and stakeholders to understand
their preferences but also to explain your approach and set expectations.
• Understand the various technology methodologies involved in each a

minology with team members and stakeholders to understand
their preferences but also to explain your approach and set expectations.
• Understand the various technology methodologies involved in each approach and
their relative advantages. These methodologies involve technology-specific ones —
for example, eXtreme Development — and others that are more general in nature —
for example, Design for Six Sigma.8
• Be practical. At times, using different communication styles with different constituents
for the same project might be more appropriate and effective.

Every problem, whether it is related to technology or organizational design, can and should
be considered along a number of relevant dimensions. Architects, whether they are developing
technology architectures or organizational architectures, constantly look at the issues from
various angles to understand them fully. Each of these angles, or views, provides an insight
into the overall problem space. Using views greatly simplifies the architecture definition
process.

Each of these dimensions is critical and interdependent. But each needs to be looked
at separately as well, using separate views for each domain. Each of these views must be con-
sistent with one another and each is relevant to specific types of technologists with specific
focus, such as end-user presentation architects and data architects.
There are also other types of domains that are equally important and require separate
views, such as governance, process and organization. In addition, as we will see in the next
section, you can look at these domains at various levels of specificity, such as conceptual,
logical and physical.
Combining these domains provides a convenient way of describing the scope of a
particular set of issues and solutions of the overall problem space. For example:
• Application architects who develop the high-level structure of the ERP solution need to
start looking at what the “conceptual x application architecture” view of the project is.
• IT operations people who are concerned about how

velop the high-level structure of the ERP solution need to
start looking at what the “conceptual x application architecture” view of the project is.
• IT operations people who are concerned about how the data centers will verify that
the ERP solution is properly deployed need to consider issues pertaining to the combined
“physical layer x process x computing infrastructure” views of the ERP project.
The overall architecture can be expressed as a series of views that help various audiences
and stakeholders understand what the architecture is. It’s important to note that each of
these views is necessary, but once they have been recognized as relevant, each of them
should be considered. One of the jobs of the chief architect is to identify what domains/views
are relevant and to make sure that they are then taken into consideration and analyzed.9

implemented with the appropriate tools on the right infrastructure and consistent with

fIGure 3 The Concept of Views Applied to Organizational Design
summary of Key Ideas
• IT solutions and IT organizational models are too complex to be considered as a whole.
Approaching their design through various angles or views helps manage complexity.
Views are interrelated but self-contained.
• For each problem, there are a number of views that can be considered. But whether
designing an IT or organizational architecture, make sure you identify the views that
are relevant to the environment.
• You can try to prioritize views, but be ready to review these choices later. For example,
what might initiall

nal architecture, make sure you identify the views that
are relevant to the environment.
• You can try to prioritize views, but be ready to review these choices later. For example,
what might initially be thought of as a metrics problem might in fact be created by
issues in values.
A useful way of organizing views is to consider issues at three broad levels of complexity
and specificity: conceptual, logical and physical layers, as discussed in the next section.

Architects segregate issues by making a distinction between the conceptual, logical and
physical views of the issues at stake, from now on referred to as the “CLP” trilogy. Grouping
architecture activities and work products at different levels of specificity allows architects
to provide the right level of information to different users within the organization.

examples in the IT world
The distinction between the various CLP views is immensely useful when developing
technology solutions because it greatly simplifies the interaction between the various
stakeholders involved. The distinction confirms that shareholders get the information
they need and are not encumbered by information they do not need.
For example (Figure 4):
• Corporate planners rarely need anything more than access to a conceptual view
of the solution they are presented with for approval.
•

chunk_id: /Users/manuelbarbero/Developer/AIStudio/data/corpora/demo/uploads/Architecture Concepts and How To Use Them To Design an Organizations.pdf::page-14::chunk-30
doc_id: /Users/manuelbarbero/Developer/AIStudio/data/corpora/demo/uploads/Architecture Concepts and How To Use Them To Design an Organizations.pdf
source_path: /Users/manuelbarbero/Developer/AIStudio/data/corpora/demo/uploads/Architecture Concepts and How To Use Them To Design an Organizations.pdf
text:
umbered by information they do not need.
For example (Figure 4):
• Corporate planners rarely need anything more than access to a conceptual view
of the solution they are presented with for approval.
• Individuals concerned with the dissemination of knowledge within the IT organization
so that effective and appropriate design practices (or patterns — see section 7) propagate
within the organization need to develop and share logical architectures or blueprints.
• Finally, people involved in the actual implementation of the solution need specific
plans and information, such as what is the recommended buy list, for their job
without the high overhead that a more holistic description of the requirements
would provide.
The distinction between the CLP layers also encourages that their contents are revised
with the appropriate level of frequency. Conceptual architecture is usually established at
the onset of a project for a specific initiative or only once every two or three years at the
enterprise level, while physical architecture might change quite frequently to accommodate
changes in technology used by the actual suppliers of hardware or software implemented.

Conceptual view. The conceptual view of the IT organization should succinctly encap-
sulate the general principles10 presiding over the definition of the organization’s mission,
as well as the vision of the enterprise, as to what the IT organization should aspire to be
and do. For example:
• Different organizations aspire to various degrees of self-sufficiency in controlling
the resources and the IT value chain — that is, the set of processes associated with
the production of solutions to the end users.11 Options ran

text:
e to various degrees of self-sufficiency in controlling
the resources and the IT value chain — that is, the set of processes associated with
the production of solutions to the end users.11 Options range from total insourcing to
total outsourcing. Organizations also allow various degrees of discretion in having
the business units make their own decisions to use or not use IT shared services.
• Organizations also exhibit various degrees of willingness to partner with vendors, as
opposed to considering them exclusively as providers of parts, be it hardware, software
or individuals used in staff augmentation mode.
The various combinations of options leads to conceptually very different types of
organizations (see Figure 5 for two extremes):
The factory model. In this approach, the organization treats its clients as captive customers
who can negotiate only unit cost. It has a balance sheet-heavy approach to resource management:
the IT group owns all the resources, including fixed assets, and uses a procurement-driven
relationship with its vendors. Although essentially obsolete nowadays, we still find a number
of organizations that explicitly or de facto use this conceptual model.
The se

relationship with its vendors. Although essentially obsolete nowadays, we still find a number
of organizations that explicitly or de facto use this conceptual model.
The service organization model. In this approach, which contrasts sharply with the factory
model, the IT organization tries to emulate what a service organization does, for example
by managing its client relationships through contract-like arrangements and dealing with
its vendors in a collaborative mode. In this scenario, a number of resources and assets
can be outsourced. A fundamental attribute of this type of organization is that the IT

does and present to the user community a menu of solutions in a formal way. Overall, this
IT organization adheres to the “service-oriented” model.12
The point here is that agreeing to a conceptual vision of the IT organization is a prerequisite
to the next step of the organizational design process:
Logical view. Defining the logical view of the organization is fundamentally equivalent to
defining the IT value chain, its components and how they interact. This is discussed in
more detail in sections 4 and 5, regarding IT value chain and use cases, respectively.
Physical view. Fundamentally, organizational diagrams are the physical views of the
organization. Sure, the devil is in the details, but starting at this

Physical view. Fundamentally, organizational diagrams are the physical views of the
organization. Sure, the devil is in the details, but starting at this level of specificity is
not the right way to analyze or redesign an organization.
Note that a number of different physical views can be derived from the same logical
view. For example, an IT organization’s logical view might make a distinction between two
separate functions, such as strategic vendor management and procurement, which are in
fact very different, even though obviously related.13 In practice and because of many
different factors, these functions might be supported by the same group of individuals.
