Figure 6 summarizes the principle attributes of the CLP views of the IT organization.
summary of Key Ideas
• In developing an organization or a technical design, the temptation to start by drawing
boxes and reporting arrows is enormous. This physical view of the organization is, of
course, of great importance for all involved, but it cannot and should not be developed
without prior, viable conceptual and logical views of the organization.
• The conceptual view of the organization is one that has to be proposed by executive
management and reflect its vision and values for the organization. Even though this
view might look abstract and almost simplistic, experience shows that arriving at a
clear consensus for it is no small task and the exercise of getting to that common
vision is fundamentally necessary.
• As we will see in section 6, distinguishing between defining the conceptual view of
the IT organization is tantamount to defining key principles that will preside over
the design and operation of the o

chunk_id: /Users/manuelbarbero/Developer/AIStudio/data/corpora/demo/uploads/Architecture Concepts and How To Use Them To Design an Organizations.pdf::page-16::chunk-39

text:
s we will see in section 6, distinguishing between defining the conceptual view of
the IT organization is tantamount to defining key principles that will preside over
the design and operation of the organization.
• The logical view of the organization is really where key processes are defined and
sections 4 and 5 of this point of view provide a set of tools for that purpose.
• Translating the logical view into the physical view is then almost a straightforward
exercise. This said, going from the definition of the conceptual to the logical and then
physical view is seldom a waterfall exercise. In practice, all three CLP views are defined
somewhat iteratively. This is why it is all the more important that many compromises
be achieved in the process. For example, two functions, however distinct, can end
up being performed by the same people just because of availability and practical
considerations, such as the fact that people have multiple skills. Having several
processes supported by the same group is in itself not an issue.

what Is It?
A value chain provides an overview of the business by depicting major processes that collec-
tively generate value for the organization and its clients. A value chain is a static view of the
business that changes little over time, and you should be able to depict the business in a
single view. The value chain is useful as a quick and sharp view of the business under
consideration within its environment, including suppliers, clients and even competition.
The analysis allows deeper study of the organization to determine the added value of
each organizational component and, most importantly, helps identify the value creation areas.
examples in the IT world
The objectives of developing a value chain from an IT architecture perspective can be
defined as:
• Creating a single overview that depicts the major elements of the business.
• Providing an anchor point that can be used as a launch pad for more detailed views
from a business, financial and technical perspective.

• Providing an anchor point that can be used as a launch pad for more detailed views

• Summarizing existing information regarding the “emergent” architectural condition
of the organization.
• Defining a common notation and vocabulary for more detailed business and techni-
cal architecture work.

Value chains are used in a number of situations, for example, to help develop an
inventory of applications and to start a portfolio rationalization process. Figure 7 illustrates
an example for an investment bank in which the overall enterprise value chain has been
broken down into a number of product-dependent sub-value chains for which an inventory
of existing applications is depicted prior to a portfolio rationalization exercise.
Value chain development is also the starting point to identify and develop use cases
(see the next section).
application to IT organizational Design

is a very powerful way of looking at what the organization delivers and how it operates.

Organizations should consider how their IT group(s) uses the value chain construct. This
is a very powerful way of looking at what the organization delivers and how it operates.
It is of tremendous help to examine the myriad issues associated with all aspects of the
organization design and to answer key questions, including:
• What are the key processes?
• How do key processes interact?
At the highest level, you can represent the IT value chain as shown in Figure 8.
Using the concept we defined earlier in the paper, this is a logical view of the organization.
This sample diagram indicates that the IT organization, regardless of its actual structure,
is designed to “consume” resources and technology provided by suppliers and to deliver
solutions to clients.
At the core of this prototypical view of the IT value chain are three key components,
further detailed in Figure 9:
• A vendor interface that deals with suppliers. Typically, this includes the support of
the processes associated with the strategic vendor management function, but not
necessarily the procurement function, which, as we will see, is part of the overall
administrative support function.15
• One or more solution en

solutions to users. Typically, and without jumping too fast into organizational design
considerations — which, as we will see, are implementation considerations — the
solution engines are usually organized by technology, which in general includes:
– The communication engine that deals with voice and data
communication, sometimes two distinct engines.
– The data center engine.
– The application development engine.
– The distributed computing or desktop support engine.
These “solution engines” can be supplemented by individual or cross-organizational
centers of excellence. As an organization evolves, it needs to focus on building areas
of expertise in various technology domains staffed by highly competent technologists
who would address some of the more cutting-edge, technology-related activities,
complex software issues and product/component intell

models.
An important point needs to be noted here. Even if these solution engines focus on
specific domains of the technology stack, they deliver what would amount to (in
architectural parlance, further defined in section 9, “Embracing Service Orientation”)
“common services.” For example, the infrastructure engine manages the technology
resources needed to support the other solution engines. To break this up across
individual solution engine silos would result in diluting the expertise these resources
would bring collectively as a team.
• One or several client interfaces, which provide the ultimate contact point with the
user community. Typically, these include field support, but they are also a form of
account management aimed at managing expectations and understanding user
requirements (generally aligned by business units).

and the other much less so:
• Go

The core IT value chain in Figure 8 includes two other components, one rather obvious
and the other much less so:
• Governance. This component of the value chain is self-explanatory because it deals
with the definition and monitoring of the execution of the IT vision, in accordance
with the corporate or divisional vision. Along with governance, the various admini-
strative support functions handle other functions, such as human resources and
finance, regulatory compliance, and security, and role management that help the
organization function.
We cannot underestimate the importance of developing the right governance model —
one that places the responsibility for ensuring that the various components of the
model work in a coordinated manner.
• Product management. This component of the value chain is often forgotten even
at this relatively high level of analysis. Here is the underlying idea: in order to deliver
value, the IT organization, or any other organization for that matter, needs to act
essentially as a regular service organization that delivers a set of products or services,
that is, a combinatio

chunk_id: /Users/manuelbarbero/Developer/AIStudio/data/corpora/demo/uploads/Architecture Concepts and How To Use Them To Design an Organizations.pdf::page-20::chunk-49
doc_id: /Users/manuelbarbero/Developer/AIStudio/data/corpora/demo/uploads/Architecture Concepts and How To Use Them To Design an Organizations.pdf
source_path: /Users/manuelbarbero/Developer/AIStudio/data/corpora/demo/uploads/Architecture Concepts and How To Use Them To Design an Organizations.pdf
text:
ver
value, the IT organization, or any other organization for that matter, needs to act
essentially as a regular service organization that delivers a set of products or services,
that is, a combination of technology and supporting activities that the user community
is willing to buy.16
All of this requires understanding user demand and making sure that your offering
is competitive, organized into a cogent catalog and anticipates demand, among many
other factors. In fact, our point of view is that the efficient IT group is a service organi-
zation that should have organizational attributes of a full-fledged service organization.
This combination of activities and this concept have been used ever since marketing
has been considered a key function within the enterprise. The name of this function
is product management.17
16 This is true even if the user community consists essentially of captive clients (i.e., if the business units cannot procure IT
services and solutions directly from other vendors and are only supported by the internal IT organization).
17 Product management is the generic term used by marketing professionals to characterize the set of processes required
to organize a

r vendors and are only supported by the internal IT organization).
17 Product management is the generic term used by marketing professionals to characterize the set of processes required
to organize and modify the scope and nature of what an organization produces and markets. As indicated in section 9,
product management for IT organizations requires a proactive, systematic approach that takes into account user demand,
the overall environment of the firm in the IT organization and client domains, including balancing the requirements
dictated by the firm strategy and internal production constraints. Since an IT organization produces a set of services and
solutions, the temptation is great to use the term services management instead. Unfortunately, services management in
IT refers to the overall set of activities required to deliver services to users. Services management includes activities as
diverse as IT asset, fault, change, demand or service-level agreement management. It also includes activities related to the
definition of the catalog of services delivered to end users, something also found in product management as defined in this
paper. Also, be aware that the term product te

des activities related to the
definition of the catalog of services delivered to end users, something also found in product management as defined in this
paper. Also, be aware that the term product tends to be interpreted by technologists as a package application. The term
product management is used in this paper despite those ambiguities.

The same logical design can lead to very different physical implementations
depending on which additional principles are applied.
Product management is the discipline that ties in and balances the conflicting
requirements of:
– Users, advocated by the user interface functions.
– Production, the various in- or outsourced functions involved in delivering
the actual services.
– Vendors, as they are understood by the vendor management function.
This component of the model deserves a separate discussion, which you can find in
section 9, “Embracing Service Orientation.” S

text:
es.
– Vendors, as they are understood by the vendor management function.
This component of the model deserves a separate discussion, which you can find in
section 9, “Embracing Service Orientation.” Suffice it to say that we have just accomplished
using the value chain construct to start organizing our thoughts in a way that, in combination
with the use of the rest of our toolbox, will help resolve a number of issues. In particular, we
introduce the concept of product management, which we believe is an essential and often
missing part of the IT value chain.
As we observed in the previous section, the same logical design can lead to very different
physical implementations depending on which additional principles, as defined in section
6, are applied. Take, for example, the case of an organization that essentially insources all
of its IT activity, using a full factory model — that is, the IT organization owns and operates
all elements of the value chain. This can be contrasted with an organization that outsources
key elements of the solution engines — for example, the operation of its networks. These two
organizations use the same value chain model but implement it very differently b

text:
ganization that outsources
key elements of the solution engines — for example, the operation of its networks. These two
organizations use the same value chain model but implement it very differently because
different principles are applied.
Detailing the value chain (Figure 9) helps trigger the right discussion as to how each
component of the value chain should be managed. This is all the more helpful once we get
into a more granular analysis of decomposing each of the broad processes into several sub-
processes.
In brief, this granular analysis model takes the high-level view of Figure 8 and adds
a level of detail that makes it what can be termed the logical view of the IT value chain
because, with this degree of granularity, it is now possible to provide a description of how
each component of the value chain interacts. Note that we still have not yet taken this model
and attempted to define an actual organizational chart, which will be our physical view of
the organization.

The main additions to the model in Figure 9 are:
• At the user interface, we distinguish between two functions: client relationship and
site management, level 1 support. The latter is the classical break/fix field support
function. The former is a function that is rarely implemented, or implemented
correctly. It represents the activity in which the IT organization interacts with the
user community, considered as a client — a function very much akin to account
managem

text:
implemented, or implemented
correctly. It represents the activity in which the IT organization interacts with the
user community, considered as a client — a function very much akin to account
management.
• For each solution engine, we distinguish the engineering/integration, production/
operations, project management and deployment planning processes, while defining
the architecture processes at that level. The architecture processes are the activities
that verify the product management vision is translated into a coherent set of actual
deliverables produced by the solution engines from inception at the engineering
level to delivery at the operation level.
summary of Key Ideas
• Value chain representation is a very helpful technique to map out the various proc-
esses of the IT organization and to define what each component of the organization
does and how it interacts with other components, without introducing the notion of
hierarchy or reporting considerations.
• Discussing the value chain of an IT organization, or any organization, makes it possible
to develop a reasonably complete inventory of what the organization should do, as
well as what kind of interaction it should suppor

chain of an IT organization, or any organization, makes it possible
to develop a reasonably complete inventory of what the organization should do, as
well as what kind of interaction it should support internally and externally, in a way
that can be made less intimidating for all involved.

• In going through the exercise of developing the target IT value chain, it is important
not to merely replicate the current status. In fact, correctly mapping the value chain
will require thinking out of the box.
• The proposed reference IT value chain presented in this section is well established
to be generic and complete enough to apply to most IT organizations. It can be used
as a starting point for discussion. It also scales well, because any subset of the IT
organization tends to require the same processes.
• Any subset of an IT organization consumes input from either vendors or other groups
and then delivers

also scales well, because any subset of the IT
organization tends to require the same processes.
• Any subset of an IT organization consumes input from either vendors or other groups
and then delivers its output through a user interface. However primitive these processes
might be, the notions of product management, governance and others are always
present, even for smaller groups within the IT organization.
• The proposed reference value chain highlights the important product management
function, which is often ignored by IT organizational planners. Its role and the product
management and service orientation concepts are further developed in section 9,
“Embracing Service Orientation.”
• Value chain analysis is well understood by the business units, and the concept is also
widely used in the context of ERP implementation, so technologists are likely to
recognize a familiar concept there too.

A use case is a description of a flow of events that provides value to an actor, the user/customer.
Use cases capture the dynamics of a business process, as well as the interactions between
underlying elements, such a

text:
escription of a flow of events that provides value to an actor, the user/customer.
Use cases capture the dynamics of a business process, as well as the interactions between
underlying elements, such as events and services. A use case is documented by providing
the name of the participants and describing how they interact in a particular situation.
examples in the IT world
Take the value chain for a financial firm that focuses on lending to students. As we saw
earlier, the value chain depicts the relationship between the major activities and processes.
In the value chain in Figure 10, the central chevrons represent the major process of the
financial firm, from processing an application to servicing the loan, and the additional
threads represent the supporting functions.
0223

A use case is a description of a flow of events that provides value to an
actor, the user/customer. Use cases capture the dynamics of a business
process, as well as the interactions between underlying elements, such
as events and services.
The value chain provides a static view of the business that changes little over time.
In contrast, use cases drill down from the value chain and provide information

chunk_id: /Users/manuelbarbero/Developer/AIStudio/data/corpora/demo/uploads/Architecture Concepts and How To Use Them To Design an Organizations.pdf::page-24::chunk-59

source_path: /Users/manuelbarbero/Developer/AIStudio/data/corpora/demo/uploads/Architecture Concepts and How To Use Them To Design an Organizations.pdf
text:
ments, such
as events and services.
The value chain provides a static view of the business that changes little over time.
In contrast, use cases drill down from the value chain and provide information regarding
interactions that support the activities. The combination of all use cases provides a dynamic
view of the business, for example, the “Apply for Loan” use case helps understand specifi-
cally what happens during loan applications.
From a practical standpoint, there are a number of ways to actually document a use
case, either in textual form, pictorially or using a combination of both approaches.18
A convenient way of showing the actual interaction of actors, either people or system
(on top of the diagram), over a period of time (the arrow of time going from top to bottom)
is achieved through, for example, what is known as a “swim lane” diagram, accompanied
by a textual description of what is going on. Figure 11 is the depiction of the “Apply for Loan”
use case.
application to IT organizational Design
The relevance of use case as a device to structure any process and test the resilience of a
system (defined as a combination of organization, process and technology) to support t

text:
organizational Design
The relevance of use case as a device to structure any process and test the resilience of a
system (defined as a combination of organization, process and technology) to support those
key processes has immediate application in the design and implementation of the IT organi-
zation (or any organization for that matter).
What we suggest here is that what the organization architect has to do is:
• Identify the key activities the IT organization has to support. There are usually
two types of use cases: those that directly touch the users and those that are
necessary to support the organization itself.19
• Develop the related use case. For example, Figure 12 presents a list of use cases
that were developed to support infrastructure-related activities of a typical IT
organization.
Use cases should be driven by a combination of the business vision/drivers and
technology goals of the organization. The joint business vision/drivers and technology goals
will help focus on areas of activities that may significantly impact the way the organization
needs to be (re)designed.

the Unified Modeling Language (UML) — the “swim lane” construct used in this paper is a UML concept.
19 Technologists make a subtle distinction between scenario and use case, the former being a generic description of the latter.
Considering that the term use case is somewhat nonintuitive, the term scenario might be easier to use with a non-technologist
audience.

Application typically completed and submitted by the student, school or guarantor. This process can be accomplished through several channels, such as Internet, fax or letter.
The following actions can occur in any order and must be completed for loan approval:
•

text:
school or guarantor. This process can be accomplished through several channels, such as Internet, fax or letter.
The following actions can occur in any order and must be completed for loan approval:
• Validate certification information sent in by the school. This is done for student loans only. The certification information consists of financials, school information, loan
dates, etc. This information is matched and validated against the student application. The certification information from the school can be received via the Internet,
Common-Line or letter/fax, phone or manual application. The lender may request the certification from the school or the school may initiate the process.
• Match loan program entered by student to loan program offered by guarantor/lender combination (student loans).
• Obtain loan guarantee from guarantor, if applicable (electronic or manual). For certain loans, blanket or default guarantees apply that make it unnecessary to get explicit
approval from the guarantor. However, the guarantor is informed of the loan (guarantees are not obtained for private loans).
• Obtain credit rating from credit bureau (e.g., non-guaranteed loans). The interaction with

guarantor. However, the guarantor is informed of the loan (guarantees are not obtained for private loans).
• Obtain credit rating from credit bureau (e.g., non-guaranteed loans). The interaction with the credit bureau can be electronic or manual. Input to obtain credit bureau report
is done manually. Output is generated electronically online or in the form of a paper report.
• Perform risk analysis and assign rates.
• Obtain underwriting (non-guaranteed loans).
• Perform exception processing, such as credit rejection, application changes, missing borrower information. All exception processing activities have to be communicated
to the student, lender and guarantor where appropriate.
• Obtain final approval and inform appropriate parties or notify borrower of denied loan.

text:
DIsTInGuIshInG BeTween PrIncIPLes, sTanDarDs anD ImPLemenTaTIon consIDeraTIons
what Is It?
Architects segregate issues by making a distinction between principles, standards and
implementation considerations and have the associated decisions made by different people,
at different times, using different approaches.
examples in the IT world
Two examples of this approach in the technology realm are:
• In the system integration realm, deciding to use open source architecture can be
construed as a first-level principle, adopting a general SOA approach to architect
applications is essentially tantamount to adopting a selected subset of the alphabet
soup of SOA-related standards (SOAP, UDDI, WSDL), and implementation considera-
tions would then include deciding whether to use pure open source tools or
commercial implementation packages in the field.20
• An organization might decide that, as a principle, the enterprise should use a common
shared infrastructure to communicate, using TCP/IP as a common set of protocols
for a standard. Selecting specific equipment vendors becomes an implementation
consideration.

and degree of specificity/applicability commensurate with and relevant to the problem at
hand. For example, a new CIO might start his tenure by proposin

text:
ted at the onset of many projects, with a scope
and degree of specificity/applicability commensurate with and relevant to the problem at
hand. For example, a new CIO might start his tenure by proposing and promoting very high-
level principles aimed at energizing the organization and making sure that these principles
are consistent with the organization’s objectives and values.
The distinction between principles, standards and implementation considerations is
also necessary to implement the governance model for the IT organization, in essence:
• Principles, just like the conceptual view of the organization, should be promoted and
enforced by the executive level of the organization and should rarely be modified.
Enforcement is usually performed through an ad hoc committee (the IT steering
committee) convened by the CEO or CIO, managed by the CIO, and involving business
unit leaders as key stakeholders, helped by each business unit technology leader
(if such a position exists).
These principles will have to be correlated explicitly to business benefits for the com-
pany to gain from their adoption21 and should be validated by the IT steering committee.
Typically, such a group should

text:
nciples will have to be correlated explicitly to business benefits for the com-
pany to gain from their adoption21 and should be validated by the IT steering committee.
Typically, such a group should make decisions such as “There will be no more than
a limited number of database standards throughout the firm” or “We need a
corporatewide WAN.”
• Standards are best left to the community of technology architects throughout the
firm, organized into a distributed virtual team (or communities of interests) taking
their directions from the principle-setting group. Led by a senior architect reporting
to the CIO (or one of the key technology leaders for a business unit most impacted
by the standard under consideration), this standards committee should edit and
publish reference documents that can then help the implementation community
define the specifics of the implementation.
For example, a standards committee involved in system integration would publish a
developer guidebook with the following information: recommended (and deprecated)
patterns, criteria for tool selection and use, construction guidelines for applications
and wrappers, cross-environment integration techniques, and example

Defining an organizational architecture/model requires the discipline usually
associated with the architecture of complex systems.
• Implementation considerations should be adapted for various groups involved in
the implementation of the standards.
In the system integration example, implementation considerations include the selection
or development of workgroup repositories, construction tools, editors, linkers, compilers,
debuggers, emulators, the selection and management of libraries (DLLs, OCXs, classes,
templates, etc.), selection and setup of the testing tools for scripting, results capture
and analysis, software change management (includes configuration management),
and process and project management.
Note that all of the above could change from project to project, that is relatively often,
something that management often resists, but programmers insist on because they

Note that all of the above could change from project to project, that is relatively often,
something that management often resists, but programmers insist on because they
believe in “using the right tool for the job.”
application to IT organizational Design
Defining an organizational architecture/model requires the discipline usually associated with
the architecture of complex systems. This requires the definition of the principles, standards
and implementation considerations that collectively define an architecture. As indicated in
footnote 10, there is one-to-one mapping between the conceptual/logical/physical concepts
and the principle/standard/implementation considerations concepts:
• Principles govern decisions made and actions taken. Just like IT principles preside
over the definition of IT conceptual architectures, organizational principles preside
over the definition of the conceptual view of the organization.
For example, principles related to the use of outsourcing influence the positioning
of the IT organization along the factory/service-oriented continuum presented in
section 3. The development and acceptance of principles then influences t

chunk_id: /Users/manuelbarbero/Developer/AIStudio/data/corpora/demo/uploads/Architecture Concepts and How To Use Them To Design an Organizations.pdf::page-31::chunk-81

source_path: /Users/manuelbarbero/Developer/AIStudio/data/corpora/demo/uploads/Architecture Concepts and How To Use Them To Design an Organizations.pdf
text:
use of outsourcing influence the positioning
of the IT organization along the factory/service-oriented continuum presented in
section 3. The development and acceptance of principles then influences the
standards selected.
• A standard in IT is a common or formally adopted method for accomplishing
something. Various standards may exist, and the choice of one over another is
determined by the principles adopted by an organization.
In terms of the IT organization, standards preside over the development of the
logical view of the organization.
For example, one organizational standard can be to always organize a competitive
bidding process in dealing with vendors above a specified threshold of expense.

From a practical standpoint, the notion of standard is closely related to the concept
of guideline. Guidelines are a subset of the standards that the organization strives
to achieve, although sometimes exce

chunk_id: /Users/manuelbarbero/Developer/AIStudio/data/corpora/demo/uploads/Architecture Concepts and How To Use Them To Design an Organizations.pdf::page-31::chunk-82

source_path: /Users/manuelbarbero/Developer/AIStudio/data/corpora/demo/uploads/Architecture Concepts and How To Use Them To Design an Organizations.pdf
text:
practical standpoint, the notion of standard is closely related to the concept
of guideline. Guidelines are a subset of the standards that the organization strives
to achieve, although sometimes exceptions need to be made. The organization should
capture these and document exceptions wherever they diverge from the guidelines
so that they can revisit it constantly as it evolves. This type of situation often appears
in the applications realm in which a guideline could be to the effect that all technol-
ogies used should adhere to open standards. This is something most organizations
strive to achieve but cannot, due to practical considerations.
• Implementation considerations need to be defined to make standards actionable.
Taking the previous example (use of competitive bidding as a standard), defining the
minimum number of bidders and the level at which a competitive bidding process
is required (i.e., above $50,000) are implementation considerations.
Case Study 2 illustrates the use of principles, standards and implementation considera-
tions concepts used in the design of a new compensation program.

summary of Key Ideas
• There is nothing earth-shattering needed for IT professionals to consider principles,
standards and implementation (PSI). It is, however, not uncommon for management
to overlook how important it is to distinguish between them, thus leading to inter-
minable debates on implementation considerations issues while principles have
not been firmed up.
• The direct linkage between the PSI hierarchy and the conceptual/logical/physical
provides a way to define a governance model.
• Typically, it is executive management’s prerogative to develop a conceptual vision
of the organization by articulating key principles.
• Key stakeholders should work together with management in defining the logical
view of the organization (using the value chain and use case tools, among others),
while human resources should support the logistics of the all-too-important imple-
mentation of the results of the organizational exercise. This is somewhat simplistic
in many ways — for example, human resources should (and usually has) a major
role in facilitating the discussions i

case sTuDy 2: PrIncIPLes, sTanDarDs anD ImPLemenTaTIon consIDeraTIons for archITecTInG a new comPensaTIon ProGram
Consider the implementation of a new compensation program (CP) aimed at introducing variable compensation within a population of executives who
are currently paid using a mostly nonvariable plan. In architecting the new CP, the organization has to adopt three perspectives (or “views”):
1. conceptual. In order to make sure that the plan accomplishes the business objectives, the new compensation plan architects have to make
sure that they spell out the key principles that will be used in designing the plan. In this particular case, some of the principles include (note: the
following is not an exhaustive list):
• Transparency. In order to promote fairness and meritocracy, the compensation of all executives will be known to all, as well as reviewed periodically
by a group of their peers.
• consistency. The CP will take into account the key level at whic

chunk_id: /Users/manuelbarbero/Developer/AIStudio/data/corpora/demo/uploads/Architecture Concepts and How To Use Them To Design an Organizations.pdf::page-32::chunk-85
doc_id: /Users/manuelbarbero/Developer/AIStudio/data/corpora/demo/uploads/Architecture Concepts and How To Use Them To Design an Organizations.pdf
source_path: /Users/manuelbarbero/Developer/AIStudio/data/corpora/demo/uploads/Architecture Concepts and How To Use Them To Design an Organizations.pdf
text:
and meritocracy, the compensation of all executives will be known to all, as well as reviewed periodically
by a group of their peers.
• consistency. The CP will take into account the key level at which activities are performed by the executives (C-level, corporate managers and line
managers) and their actual span of control, managed by business or budget, or other appropriate metrics that reflect the magnitude of the responsibility
supported.
• Partner-like model. Both the fixed and variable parts of the compensation will be evaluated using an internal currency (“unit”) whose value will
fluctuate over time as a function of the overall enterprise’s results. In addition, individual executives will be awarded an initial set of units and then
additional units as a function of current pay level (for the fixed part of the compensation) and ongoing performance (for the variable part).
• Promoting ownership. The variable part of compensation will be available optionally as a combination of cash and stock, with stock being awarded
at a discount to promote stock ownership.
2. Logical. Implementing the new CP calls for the specific definition of the constructs, that is the standards that will

at a discount to promote stock ownership.
2. Logical. Implementing the new CP calls for the specific definition of the constructs, that is the standards that will be used, for example, with
respect to the four items above. The plan will make specific provisions such as:
• setting up the compensation committees. In order to implement the bold principles described above, the logical model of the CP will have to
spell out the membership rules and operating model of the one or various committees that will actually set up and review how much units are awarded.
• setting up a banding model. To achieve the bold principles, specific rules need to be agreed on to define the “level” and “magnitude” of the
responsibility to be recognized by the CP. For example, by considering three levels (line, management and executive) and four types of magnitude
(for example, budget under control of executives in support functions or sales for those in charge of specific delivery), the compensation of all
executives will be mapped into a three by four matrix with distinct fixed/variable compensation schemes.

examples in the IT world
Since time immemorial, every developer worth his or her salt has had his or her own toolset,
be it tested code snippets or well-honed techniques he or she uses systematically.
The concept of patterns has been recognized formally and explicitly in the system
integration realm at least since the early 1990s, when developers — within their group, then
within their enterprise and finally across the whole community — decided to share their
wisdom and results and publish catalogs of useful patterns, essentially a collection of
well-documented effective practices.24
Today, IT strategy and architecture problems are usually solved through analysis and
decision making influenced by what architects have either read or directly experienced.
Architecture patterns assist architects in this decision-making process by helping to identify
the various options for solving a given architecture problem, understand the variations and
work with trade-offs. So architectural patterns have emerged, too.

the various options for solving a given architecture problem, understand the variations and
work with trade-offs. So architectural patterns have emerged, too. These patterns evolve
along with the business problems they address and with architects’ own experiences.25

application to IT organizational Design
Organizations often talk about documenting leading practices, using reusable documentation
or artifacts, or using standardized processes when designing their organizational model.
Here are a few relatively trivial examples: Take the “task force” approach, an organi-
zational design tool, or “multiple reporting,” an organizational design construct.
Both “task force” and “multiple reporting” can be considered patterns (and as such
capitalized as “Task Force” and “Multiple Reporting”), in that they are solutions that can be
used to address certain problems (i.e., Task Force for decision making and Multiple Reporting
to enforce multigroup collaboration or share multifaceted resources).
Organizational architects should also leverage the lessons learned in the technology
realm. They should mine and document patterns and define their applicability.
The Multiple Reporting or Task Force pattern results can be useful provided that they
are used in the right context, a context whose explicit description should be detailed in the
pattern definition. For example, by looking at the historical recor

chunk_id: /Users/manuelbarbero/Developer/AIStudio/data/corpora/demo/uploads/Architecture Concepts and How To Use Them To Design an Organizations.pdf::page-34::chunk-92

source_path: /Users/manuelbarbero/Developer/AIStudio/data/corpora/demo/uploads/Architecture Concepts and How To Use Them To Design an Organizations.pdf
text:
sults can be useful provided that they
are used in the right context, a context whose explicit description should be detailed in the
pattern definition. For example, by looking at the historical record, an organization might
conclude that Multiple Reporting does not work unless it is initiated by the individual con-
cerned, or that it can only work when applied to an individual below (or above) a certain
level of autonomy. Another organization (think Dilbert’s world) might decide to stay away
altogether from using Task Force based on the historical understanding that it doesn’t work.
Overall, organizational architects should leverage the concept of pattern, a construct
well understood by technologists to define, organize and document leading organizational
practices and standards. In addition, IT architects have at times identified anti-patterns,
approaches that are known to fail, so that they can be avoided in the future. Task Force
might have proven to be such an anti-pattern in many a company.
summary of Key Ideas
• Recognizing patterns is a way for any organization to become a learning organization,
whatever the topic at hand.
• The notion of pattern has a long history in the f

summary of Key Ideas
• Recognizing patterns is a way for any organization to become a learning organization,
whatever the topic at hand.
• The notion of pattern has a long history in the field of software development but is
rarely used elsewhere as such, although it is essentially another way of defining and
documenting leading practices.
• Sometimes, recognizing patterns for behavior, concepts or constructs that do not
work (so-called anti-patterns) is just as critical.

The ImPorTance of PLaTeau DefInITIon
what Is It?
Key challenges when defining a viable IT architecture include:
• Requirements and the environment changing considerably.
• Lack of a good baseline of what exists (the current state).
• An enormous amount of uncertainty ahead, which might make the definition of a
precise end state an exercise in pure speculative futility.
If we try to design an organization using a waterfall approach to problem solving, we
find out that a considerable amount of energy can be spent in defining the current state in
a way that is rarely used later on (because the description becomes either obsolete, just too
unwieldy or both). Much effort is also spent deliberating as to what the end state will look
like, only to realize that there are so many unknowns that just about any end state could be
a goal. That leaves the organization in analysis paralysis with no clear idea as to how to get
started.
The situation is mildly better if the organization uses an iterative approach, but it
will similarly be bogged down if it insists on thoroughly documenting the current state
and defining the end state with precision. In both cases, defi

chunk_id: /Users/manuelbarbero/Developer/AIStudio/data/corpora/demo/uploads/Architecture Concepts and How To Use Them To Design an Organizations.pdf::page-35::chunk-95

text:
e organization uses an iterative approach, but it
will similarly be bogged down if it insists on thoroughly documenting the current state
and defining the end state with precision. In both cases, defining a real transition plan is
practically impossible.
Instead, for an organization that wants to get from A to C, we recommend using a pro-
gressive and pragmatic approach that defines realistic “transformation plateaus” (B1, B2,
B3, etc.) aimed at implementing the end state step by step, while reducing technology and
implementation risks (Figure 14).
Here is how it works:
• Spend minimal time defining the current state. Since most of this will be changed
and the initial changes will be incremental, everyone involved will start with a pretty
good idea of where they stand without having to fill large binders of descriptive
information that will remain shelved and unused going forward.
• Define a high-level view of the end state, but stay at the conceptual level and
refrain from going to a higher level of precision. The information needed to achieve
precision is probably nonexistent. In some cases, it is known that the technology
necessary to implement that vision is not ready yet, budg

precision is probably nonexistent. In some cases, it is known that the technology
necessary to implement that vision is not ready yet, budgets are not available or key
talents have not been recruited yet, so the end state can and should remain defined
at the conceptual level.

The challenge lies in developing an actionable plan to reach
the end state (C) from the current state (A).
• Define various implementation plateaus along the way, but focus on executing
the very first plateaus. Only the next and possibly the next plateau after that should
be defined in detail (e.g., down to the physical level). We know that things are going
to change and do not want to create detailed plans that are likely to change for the
plateaus further along in the road map to the end state.
Implementation plateaus are stepping stones that help us reach the desired end state
in actionable increments:
• Each plateau should be built with technology that exists at the initiation point.
No future promises in that regard should be counted on.
• Each plateau should provide a business benefit that must be clearly articulated with
metrics and a scorecard to assess the journey while supporting the business case.
• Each plateau should have a duration of approximately three to six months; there
should be enough time to deliver, but the end of the tunnel should also be visible
from the outset.
• When reaching a plateau, one should reasses

and/or make sure that the new plateau the organization is embarking on will still be
directionally correct.

application to IT organizational Design
The overall idea is to develop implementation plateaus as stepping stones allowing us to
reach the desired end state in actionable increments, with emphasis on the first plateau.
In essence, what we have described is a variation on the “iterative/low ceremony”
theme presented in the first section of this point of view, “Understanding ‘Ceremony’ and
the ‘Iterative/Waterfall’ Trade-Offs,” with additional comments on the futility of spending
too much time on the description of the current and end state. Note that, in this context,
developing a transition plan consists of documenting the activities required to address the
gaps between plateaus at each transition.
summary of Key Ideas
To avoid the anti-patterns that plague a number of reorganization projects:
• Do not confuse small random walk and incremental implementation. Most organi-
zations are reluctant to implement radical changes in the organization and tend to
make only incremental changes. The issue here is that, unless these are oriented
toward an end state, this is perceived, and indeed is, wasted Brownian motion.
• Do not focus on the present and the familiar. Spendin

text:
mental changes. The issue here is that, unless these are oriented
toward an end state, this is perceived, and indeed is, wasted Brownian motion.
• Do not focus on the present and the familiar. Spending too much time defining
the current state is rarely useful.
• Recognize that, at times, the future will no longer be what it used to be. The end
state should indeed be defined, but only conceptually. And, most importantly, this
vision of the future should be revised periodically, at least every time a plateau has
been reached.
• Avoid analysis paralysis. Only the next immediate plateau can and should be
analyzed in detail.

Here is the logical link: Let’s first state the obvious: a service is what an entity (a person,
an organization, a piece of software27) requests or receives when interacting with another
entity. A service-oriented “construct”28 is one that, when looked at on the conceptual and
logical level, shows that entities interact through a collection of well-defined, self-contained,
(ideally) self-describing interfaces and does not depend on the context or state of other
services.
By that definition, anything but the most monolithic constructs use services. A service-
oriented construct is one that essentially functions through a limited collection of specialized
services exchanging well-defined information (or goods).

soa in the IT world
An SOA is an architecture where each application module or data repository connects to one
another through well-defined interfacing mechanisms. The communication can involve
either simple data passing or two or more services coordinating an activity. Some means
of connecting services to each other is needed.
A canonical example of service in the context of application development would be
“authentication services.” An SOA that implements authentication services provides devel-
opers with a common way of making sure that anyone developing a new application that calls
for users to log in can insert a simple set of commands in their code that will call a single
program (the one supporting the SOA service) anytime the software needs to authenticate
the user. This authentication software is developed, maintained and upgraded in a centralized
fashion so that software developers do not have to reinvent the wheel every time they need
that functionality in their program.

application to IT organizational Design
The SOA discussion shows something that is intuitively obvious: Entities (be they users or
software) tend to interact better when what they get and receive is well defined and obtained
through well-defined interfaces. If we consider the IT organization as a whole, that means
that each functional element of the value chain should deliver specific services to the other
elements of the value chain. Figure 15 shows various components of the value chain proposed
in section 4 of this paper and the essential services they provide to one another. In fact, each
component of the value chain could be considered a “service.”

examples in the IT world and IT organizational Design
Even a superficial description of the Six Sigma philosophy is out of the scope of this point of
view, but a very brief introduction to how Six Sigma approaches (patterns) and tools apply to
technology-oriented and organizational architecture projects is possible.
Generally, the Six Sigma toolset should be considered when dealing with an issue that
is neither trivial nor necessarily well defined and/or when the issues at stake are important
and cut across organizational boundaries, which is typically true for both IT and IT reorgani-
zation projects.
Most people are aware of the use of the Six Sigma concepts and toolset for the purpose
of existing process improvement or management, mostly by using statistical analysis and
other data-oriented techniques (Figure 19). This is usually accomplish

text:
igma concepts and toolset for the purpose
of existing process improvement or management, mostly by using statistical analysis and
other data-oriented techniques (Figure 19). This is usually accomplished using a combination
of key steps — Define, Measure, Analyze, Improve, Implement and Control — referred to
collectively as the DMAIIC steps.
Most people do not realize that there is another path through the Six Sigma world,
aimed at defining new processes, an approach that can be used if a process does not even
exist, if incremental improvement is not enough or if what is needed is an entirely new
process, product, service or solution.

Texere, 2000, ISBN 1-58799-00-8, where Robert G. Hadstrom shows how investors can gain insight from the work of others in
very different fields.

Discussions:
Design for Six Sigma: Process/Product Design
0423

In the Six Sigma framework, defining an entirely new set of processes requires a new
approach, based on the execution of a new set of different action steps — Define, Assess,
Select, Design, Implement and Control — referred to collectively as the Design for Six Sigma
(DFSS) approach. As the step names imply, DFSS implies that, in solving an architectural
or organizational set of problems, several solutions will be assessed and one will be selected
and implemented.
Along with the Six Sigma philosophy comes a wealth of tools that can be used in both
contexts, for example:
• Voice of the Customer analysis. The “Voice of XXX” approach/pattern helps formalize
what key stakeholders “XXX” specifically need and want. For example, in defining a
new organizational architecture (or a

text:
of the Customer analysis. The “Voice of XXX” approach/pattern helps formalize
what key stakeholders “XXX” specifically need and want. For example, in defining a
new organizational architecture (or a new compensation/incentive plan, see Case
Study 2), a voice of the employee analysis is very important to conduct.
• Quality function development. QFD is a general approach that helps both prioritize
objectives and trace how solutions can also be prioritized by how well they address
the prioritized objectives. By introducing traceability from solutions to objectives,
QFD really helps technology and organizational designers focus on what matters.31
• Tollgate analysis. As is typical for high ceremony/waterfall approaches, DFSS
recommends that going from one step to another is subject to the formal presentation
of results obtained so far. In practice, the Define phase is probably the most important
step in an organizational architecture.
• Sound Define phase. DFSS requires that the Define phase include the development
of key artifacts, which are really the ones that one would require at the outset of any
kind of well-defined project, including:
– Project mission
– Work plan
– Risk manage

By sharing our “universal architecture toolset,” we hope to help debunk both
the notions that technology architects are the high priests of an inscrutable
religion and that organizational design is exclusively a matter of insightful
improvisation.
summary of Key Ideas
• The architecture language is a creole that has absorbed and will continue to absorb
contributions from many cultures. This phenomenon has been recognized by technol-
ogists, who have, for example, embraced the object-oriented paradigm, initially
developed

chunk_id: /Users/manuelbarbero/Developer/AIStudio/data/corpora/demo/uploads/Architecture Concepts and How To Use Them To Design an Organizations.pdf::page-45::chunk-122

text:
and will continue to absorb
contributions from many cultures. This phenomenon has been recognized by technol-
ogists, who have, for example, embraced the object-oriented paradigm, initially
developed for artificial intelligence and later used in the software development
realm. The same approach has been true for the Six Sigma toolset.
• Staying alert to and intellectually curious about new paradigms and tools that can
help identify issues and model solutions is something that technology and organi-
zational architects want and need to do to continue to connect the dots.
concLusIon
We hope that the description of the 10 approaches we covered in this point of view will benefit
both architects and non-architects:
• For non-architects, the point of view can serve as a high-level primer on how to
design an architecture, whether a technology architecture or an organizational
architecture, and how architects think and work.
• For architects, the point of view can provide a way for them to contribute actively
to the debate on how to structure the IT organization.
This point of view is by no means an exhaustive exposé showing the similitude between
the two domains. For example, we did not c

chunk_id: /Users/manuelbarbero/Developer/AIStudio/data/corpora/demo/uploads/Architecture Concepts and How To Use Them To Design an Organizations.pdf::page-45::chunk-123
doc_id: /Users/manuelbarbero/Developer/AIStudio/data/corpora/demo/uploads/Architecture Concepts and How To Use Them To Design an Organizations.pdf
source_path: /Users/manuelbarbero/Developer/AIStudio/data/corpora/demo/uploads/Architecture Concepts and How To Use Them To Design an Organizations.pdf
text:
tribute actively
to the debate on how to structure the IT organization.
This point of view is by no means an exhaustive exposé showing the similitude between
the two domains. For example, we did not cover the all-important issues related to how to
secure funding to implement either. We noted that we know that ROI calculation is not really
applicable as an approach to justify investment, but every reorganization effort requires fund-
ing. One approach is for both business and technology to jointly fund this effort so that it is
seen as an enterprisewide initiative as opposed to an IT initiative.
These 10 concepts are essentially transposable almost “as is” from the IT architecture
to the organization architecture realms.
That is not to say that architecting an organization is any less an art than architecting
a technology solution is. In both cases, good judgment, experience and intuition have a role
to play. But no artist denies the importance of technique in supporting inspiration. By sharing
our “universal architecture toolset,” we hope to help debunk both the notions that technology

an enterprise architecture.Such an architecture includes
descriptive models (defined in business and technology
terms) to aid decision-makers in understanding the com-
plexities regarding how the entity operates today and how
it wants to operate in the future.It also includes prescrip-
tive models designed to help organizations successfully
transition to their desired IT environment.

elcome to the risk, compliance and security issue of Financial Services Technology Journal.
Recently, one of my colleagues described to me what some view as being the big issue in compliance
right now. In his words, here is what some clients are asking for: “Give me really good brakes and a heads-
up display and a helmet so I can drive as fast as I can, push as much product through the pipe as I can, and
have the tools to know when to slow down. In short, give me the mechanisms so that I don’t drive over the
cliff—for example, find my name in the paper, get fined three hundred million dollars or get a letter from
the office of the attorney general.”
Another equally colorful way at looking at the emergence of risk, compliance and security (RCS) man-
agement is as a major theme of the century using the TLA (three-letter acronym) paradigm so in vogue
nowadays, it looks like the RCS trifecta is really a new form of FUD (fear, uncertainty and doubt). But
should the FUD factor alone be driving the industry? Sure, dealing with fear, uncertainty and doubt is
a unifying theme when discussing risk, compliance and secu

chunk_id: /Users/manuelbarbero/Developer/AIStudio/data/corpora/demo/uploads/FS Journal, Edited by M. Barbero - RiskCompliance and Security - 2005.pdf::page-1::chunk-2
doc_id: /Users/manuelbarbero/Developer/AIStudio/data/corpora/demo/uploads/FS Journal, Edited by M. Barbero - RiskCompliance and Security - 2005.pdf

text:
FUD (fear, uncertainty and doubt). But
should the FUD factor alone be driving the industry? Sure, dealing with fear, uncertainty and doubt is
a unifying theme when discussing risk, compliance and security. But while the FUD factor might be
driving so many marketing initiatives targeted at information technology buyers (“Protect yourself
against<insert risk here>, buy<insert risk solution here>!”), it does not mean that technologists should lose
sight of what constitutes stronger and more useful linkages between the risk, compliance and security
domains. The fact is, managing RCS often requires leveraging the same kind of data and monitoring
the same processes across the three domains.
Indeed, over the last few years, market issues have increased business risk factors, and these, along
with expanding regulatory requirements, are compelling financial institutions and their technologists to
look at the convergence of key processes and the sharing of data that serve risk mitigation, regulatory
compliance and security management.
So, our hypothesis is that one of the key trends in financial services today is the convergence of RCS
management. As a result, we believe that financial servi

chunk_id: /Users/manuelbarbero/Developer/AIStudio/data/corpora/demo/uploads/FS Journal, Edited by M. Barbero - RiskCompliance and Security - 2005.pdf::page-1::chunk-3

text:
tory
compliance and security management.
So, our hypothesis is that one of the key trends in financial services today is the convergence of RCS
management. As a result, we believe that financial services organizations and their technologists should
chart their journey toward this convergence, and as such we decided to explore this journey thoroughly
with this issue. Risk, compliance and security management are part of a larger continuum that requires
financial services organizations to consider a range of issues, from financial management to business
continuity planning. This idea has significant consequences for IT planners. From a technology stand-
point, it means that financial services technologists need to look at all these initiatives using a holistic,
portfolio-based approach and implement point solutions to satisfy immediate business needs while
looking for architecture convergence opportunities as the business domains they support converge.
As with all our previous issues, we want to continue bringing you ideas, data and insights on these top-
ics in a way that puts technology in a true business context. The convergence of RCS management is so
broad that we needed and brou

chunk_id: /Users/manuelbarbero/Developer/AIStudio/data/corpora/demo/uploads/FS Journal, Edited by M. Barbero - RiskCompliance and Security - 2005.pdf::page-3::chunk-4

text:
want to continue bringing you ideas, data and insights on these top-
ics in a way that puts technology in a true business context. The convergence of RCS management is so
broad that we needed and brought together a large team of guides in our expedition (12 of them!). In
many cases, we had to examine issues from different perspectives (for example, from a process, applica-
tion and technology infrastructure standpoint). That made for interesting discussions to put together
the eight articles in this issue, the largest and most ambitious to date. The result is that you’ll have more
companions on your journey. And you won’t have to don a funny hat as you get near a cliff.
The BearingPoint professionals who contribute their insights here are eager to know how well they are
doing. We encourage you to offer feedback and ideas for future topics to Pierre Pureur, the journal’s
editor, and our team at FSTechJournalEditors@bearingpoint.com.
Manuel Barbero
Executive Editor, BearingPoint’s Financial Services Technology Journal

USING MONTE CARLO ANALYSIS
In an MCA, one runs a large number of
cost simulations assuming that for each
run the events that impact the outcome
occur randomly but with a preset average
frequency. Each individual simulation
run provides an outcome in a table, which
can be used to plot the expected distribu-
tion of outcomes.
Here is the method for computing the
outcome of a given simulation run,
denoted “r”: For each event that impacts
the outco

Specialized vendors of data-at-rest solu-
tions differ by technologies, approaches,
capabilities, performance and platform
support. Many support block-level
encryption on the device.
Many vendors offer hardware-based
appliances that are installed on the
input/output (I/O) channel between the
computer and storage subsystem. These
appliances deliver the best performance
(sometimes less than a 1 percent per-
formance penalty), total application
transparency, manageability of devices
and simple scalability.
Most current limitations of appliance-
based solutions revolve around the types
of storage subsystems and the channel
protocols used to exchange data between
the central processing unit (CPU) and
storage resources. Almost all support
storage area networks (SANs) and net-
work attached storage (NAS) over fiber
channels.

IT infrastructure refers to “what a firm needs
to make other business-specific IT resources
perform reliably and efficiently.” What it means
today is that IT infrastructure is everywhere!
IT infrastr

chunk_id: /Users/manuelbarbero/Developer/AIStudio/data/corpora/demo/uploads/FS Journal, Edited by M. Barbero - IT Infrastructure.pdf::page-1::chunk-1
doc_id: /Users/manuelbarbero/Developer/AIStudio/data/corpora/demo/uploads/FS Journal, Edited by M. Barbero - IT Infrastructure.pdf
source_path: /Users/manuelbarbero/Developer/AIStudio/data/corpora/demo/uploads/FS Journal, Edited by M. Barbero - IT Infrastructure.pdf
text:
IT infrastructure refers to “what a firm needs
to make other business-specific IT resources
perform reliably and efficiently.” What it means
today is that IT infrastructure is everywhere!
IT infrastructure is the set of technologies and services supporting robust and cost-effective
access to the storage, processing and distribution of information. A few years ago, the term
infrastructure was mostly used to refer to networks (“the communication infrastructure”).
But, over time, organizations have come to realize that the techniques and discipline that
had been used to improve the procurement, operation and life cycle management of
networks could provide design and implementation patterns applicable to other cross-IT
enterprise resources. Here are three examples to consider:
• The emerging XaaS or “XoD” delivery models.1 In the late 1990s, “bandwidth on demand”
was considered the future delivery model for communication services. The concept has
been extended toward offering other types of commodity-like services across the enter-
prise as if they were provided by some (internal or third-party) organization functioning
as a utility (including utility computing, the focus of this issu

chunk_id: /Users/manuelbarbero/Developer/AIStudio/data/corpora/demo/uploads/FS Journal, Edited by M. Barbero - IT Infrastructure.pdf::page-1::chunk-2
doc_id: /Users/manuelbarbero/Developer/AIStudio/data/corpora/demo/uploads/FS Journal, Edited by M. Barbero - IT Infrastructure.pdf
source_path: /Users/manuelbarbero/Developer/AIStudio/data/corpora/demo/uploads/FS Journal, Edited by M. Barbero - IT Infrastructure.pdf
text:
of commodity-like services across the enter-
prise as if they were provided by some (internal or third-party) organization functioning
as a utility (including utility computing, the focus of this issue’s first article). This trend
also explains the simultaneous emergence of metered services and the definition of
quality of services targets that are often derived from the telecommunications world.2
• Catalog-based service definition. In terms of IT offerings, communication solutions
were the first that could be defined using a limited number of distinct characteristics—
bandwidth, bit error rate (BER) and mean time to repair (MTTR)—that could be selected
from a service catalog. The rapid provisioning of predefined server capacity with well-
defined configurations selected from a service catalog is what the utility computing
model promises to deliver.
• Service management process and framework. The IT Infrastructure Library (ITIL®)3
is the most widely accepted approach to IT service management. ITIL applies to the
entire technology stack, but it was originally developed under the auspices of the
U.K. government’s Central Computer and Telecommunications Agency.
1“X as a Service” or “X

text:
gement. ITIL applies to the
entire technology stack, but it was originally developed under the auspices of the
U.K. government’s Central Computer and Telecommunications Agency.
1“X as a Service” or “X on Demand” with X ∈ {“Application”, “Storage”, “Computing”}.
2On how disaster recovery concepts such as recovery time objective (RTO) and recovery point objective (RPO)
are modern cousins of the mean time to repair concept found in the telecommunications industry, see
“The new challenges of disaster recovery technology and their impacts on computing” by Daryouche Behboudi
in this issue of the BearingPoint Financial Services Technology Journal.
3The IT Infrastructure Library (ITIL®) is a widely accepted approach to IT service management; the ITIL has just
recently been revised as ITIL Release 3.0, see http://www.itil.co.uk.
Summer 2007 1

Letter from the executive editor

From its humble beginning and association with data and voice communication services,
the enterprise infrastructure concept now encompasses computing and storage infrastruc-
ture (i.e., the entire data center realm)

text:
beginning and association with data and voice communication services,
the enterprise infrastructure concept now encompasses computing and storage infrastruc-
ture (i.e., the entire data center realm). And—if we extend the original definition of
infrastructure to encompass all IT resources that are used pretty much “as is” throughout
the enterprise—then it becomes clear that an even greater portion of the IT stack should
be considered (and managed) as an enterprise infrastructure. For example, candidate IT
in’•fre•struk•cher infrastructure also should include:
• Corporate applications such as e-mail (and possibly some enterprise resource
planning systems)
(in•fre•struk•cher)
• IT services (created using a service-oriented architecture and made available
by several applications)
• IT security, compliance and risk management tools
• Application messaging (enterprise application integration) solutions
Last but not least, it has become apparent that a large portion of data in the enterprise
should also be treated as infrastructure: the controlled but pervasive sharing of information
that creates a “single version of the truth” is the condition sine qua non to produce a single
view of t

chunk_id: /Users/manuelbarbero/Developer/AIStudio/data/corpora/demo/uploads/FS Journal, Edited by M. Barbero - IT Infrastructure.pdf::page-4::chunk-5
doc_id: /Users/manuelbarbero/Developer/AIStudio/data/corpora/demo/uploads/FS Journal, Edited by M. Barbero - IT Infrastructure.pdf
source_path: /Users/manuelbarbero/Developer/AIStudio/data/corpora/demo/uploads/FS Journal, Edited by M. Barbero - IT Infrastructure.pdf
text:
e
should also be treated as infrastructure: the controlled but pervasive sharing of information
that creates a “single version of the truth” is the condition sine qua non to produce a single
view of the customer or consistent reporting of financial information.
Making a distinction between business unit-specific services and resources (data, applica-
tion, computing and communication) on the one hand and those that can be shared as
a common IT infrastructure on the other hand is more than an interesting exercise in
taxonomy. This distinction helps leverage the lessons we learned over time in the intro-
duction, management and evolution of infrastructure-type activities. Following are some
of them, with a focus on the challenges inherently associated with the management and
promotion of the IT infrastructure concepts:
• IT infrastructure should be managed at the enterprise level with a focus on supporting
scalability and reduction of unit cost through economy of scale and scope.
• IT infrastructure projects are notoriously difficult to justify through a regular return on
investment/business case model. Any technologist who has ever tried to convince his
management to implement a ser

chunk_id: /Users/manuelbarbero/Developer/AIStudio/data/corpora/demo/uploads/FS Journal, Edited by M. Barbero - IT Infrastructure.pdf::page-4::chunk-6
doc_id: /Users/manuelbarbero/Developer/AIStudio/data/corpora/demo/uploads/FS Journal, Edited by M. Barbero - IT Infrastructure.pdf
source_path: /Users/manuelbarbero/Developer/AIStudio/data/corpora/demo/uploads/FS Journal, Edited by M. Barbero - IT Infrastructure.pdf
text:
frastructure projects are notoriously difficult to justify through a regular return on
investment/business case model. Any technologist who has ever tried to convince his
management to implement a service management framework or promoted a single
sign-on project has endured this kind of challenge: how do you really quantify savings?
The answer in many cases is: not easily.4
• Many stakeholders will point to the (negative) impacts on their business or area of
influence when implementing a cross-enterprise infrastructure. The remedy exists: it is
to make sure that these projects are sponsored at the right level to make the case based
on the competitive or strategic advantage delivered at the enterprise level so that “the
first business unit that needs to cross the rivers does not have to pay for the whole bridge.”
• IT infrastructure technologies have a distinct life cycle: early adopters usually introduce
the supporting technology outside of the usual scope of influence of the IT organization.
But successful rationalization of the infrastructure is a protracted process that can only
be performed if sponsored at the highest level of the organization (see the previous point).
4For a d

zation.
But successful rationalization of the infrastructure is a protracted process that can only
be performed if sponsored at the highest level of the organization (see the previous point).
4For a discussion of the relevance of business case applied to infrastructure-related projects and a discussion
of alternative approaches, see “Using Real Options to Mitigate Risks in Large IT Projects” by Pierre Pureur in
the May 2005 issue of the BearingPoint Financial Services Technology Journal.
2 BearingPoint

Financial Services Technology Journal
• The pricing of IT infrastructure-based services often leads to interminable debate as to
what is best for an organization. There are, however, predictable evolution patterns:
– At first, users accepted a one-of-a-kind pricing approach to the delivery of IT infrastruc-
ture solutions. (Until the mid-1980s, point-to-point digital circuits were priced based on
geographical position of the end points.)
– They then demanded metered services. (Bandwidth on demand was all the rage in
the 1990s.)
– Eventually, unit cost became so low (thanks to growth of usage) that they demanded
flat “all-you-can-eat” pricing structures. (I am sending this p

chunk_id: /Users/manuelbarbero/Developer/AIStudio/data/corpora/demo/uploads/FS Journal, Edited by M. Barbero - IT Infrastructure.pdf::page-5::chunk-8
doc_id: /Users/manuelbarbero/Developer/AIStudio/data/corpora/demo/uploads/FS Journal, Edited by M. Barbero - IT Infrastructure.pdf
source_path: /Users/manuelbarbero/Developer/AIStudio/data/corpora/demo/uploads/FS Journal, Edited by M. Barbero - IT Infrastructure.pdf
text:
Bandwidth on demand was all the rage in
the 1990s.)
– Eventually, unit cost became so low (thanks to growth of usage) that they demanded
flat “all-you-can-eat” pricing structures. (I am sending this paper from a DSL line in
Beijing, and I plan to call the United States later today using a Voice over Internet
Protocol, or VoIP, service that costs a flat $25 fee per year.)
Yes, as a famous French philosopher did not say, today’s discussion about chargeback for
shared IT infrastructure services is just “déjà-vu all over again”.
• • • • •
As we noted in the editorial section of the previous issue of this journal, IT infrastructure
is the place to start to define enterprisewide architectures as a prerequisite not only to
reduce costs but also to establish the foundation for harmonizing how the organization
uses technology to realize the synergies across business units.
This issue of the BearingPoint Financial Services Technical Journal is devoted entirely to
the discussion of infrastructure-related topics. One of the most innovative subjects we
focus on is utility computing, an architecture style that aims at making good on the claim
that computing resources can be delivered as a true c

chunk_id: /Users/manuelbarbero/Developer/AIStudio/data/corpora/demo/uploads/FS Journal, Edited by M. Barbero - IT Infrastructure.pdf::page-5::chunk-9
doc_id: /Users/manuelbarbero/Developer/AIStudio/data/corpora/demo/uploads/FS Journal, Edited by M. Barbero - IT Infrastructure.pdf
source_path: /Users/manuelbarbero/Developer/AIStudio/data/corpora/demo/uploads/FS Journal, Edited by M. Barbero - IT Infrastructure.pdf
text:
-related topics. One of the most innovative subjects we
focus on is utility computing, an architecture style that aims at making good on the claim
that computing resources can be delivered as a true commodity.
We also look at several aspects of planning, managing and operating IT infrastructure com-
ponents, including network (maintaining route diversity in a communication network), data
centers (analyzing cost and planning for capacity requirements), and storage infrastructure
(planning for capacity). We also discuss the various financial models applicable to the manage-
ment of IT infrastructure in terms of financial planning and reporting. A critical paper discusses
how to build a configuration management database (CMDB) and a service catalog and how
these activities are critically related.
Finally, two papers address risk management as applied to IT infrastructure, including how
to develop a pandemic response plan and how to select the right technologies and metrics
for a disaster recovery plan.
With a total of eight articles, all centered on IT infrastructure, this is (once again) the largest
issue we have published on a single subject. Perhaps I should have disclosed early on

recovery plan.
With a total of eight articles, all centered on IT infrastructure, this is (once again) the largest
issue we have published on a single subject. Perhaps I should have disclosed early on that
the executive editor started his career as a network engineer, which may have influenced
the level of attention that we, at BearingPoint, bring to the overall IT infrastructure realm.
Good reading!
Manuel Barbero
Executive Editor, BearingPoint Financial Services Technology Journal
A personnel and personal note: This is the first issue with Laurence Thiery as our formal
assistant editor. Laurence has been contributing enormously and in a number of ways to the
content, development and the editing of the Journal and her new title is merely a belated
but much deserved recognition of her past and continuous role.

The model is used to determine the maturity across all components of the foundational framework.
Maturity progression is evolutionary and may not be equal for each framework component since achieving logical and physical architecture
maturity at each level is used to build a foundation upon which to achieve the subsequent level.
Different weighting and maturity criteria will apply to the different components at each level (maturity will vary).

Business continuity planning is the process whereby financial
institutions ensure the maintenance or recovery of operations,
including services to customers, when confronted with adverse
events such as natural disasters, technological failures, human
error, or terrorism.

A close look at our example
A global investment bank engaged the authors to perform a cost reduction assessment and explore outsourcing opportunities for
the IT infrastructure. The CFO and CIO had agreed to a target of a 10–15 percent reduction in the annual expense baseline. During
the thre
