source_path: /Users/manuelbarbero/Developer/AIStudio/data/corpora/demo/uploads/FS Journal, Edited by M. Barbero - IT Infrastructure.pdf
text:
eduction assessment and explore outsourcing opportunities for
the IT infrastructure. The CFO and CIO had agreed to a target of a 10–15 percent reduction in the annual expense baseline. During
the three previous years, however, the baseline had already been reduced by 10–20 percent, and the bank had just performed an
inventory cleanup. In other words, there was not much else to be taken out.
Through a traditional cost reduction assessment we identified a series of opportunities that could yield 8–12 percent from the
previous year ($ to $11 million with an initial investment of $3 to $5 million). None of these opportunities were “low-hanging
fruit” because they involved contract renegoti- ations, automation of a number of support activities and some outtasking.
The explored outsourcing scenarios, on the other hand, showed potential to create more value than the internal cost takeout
scenario: a reduction of $30 million in the baseline in the fourth year for an initial investment of $30 million over the first three years.
In net present value (NPV), the scenario of complete outsourcing was valued at $100 million, while the internal cost takeout scenario
was valued at $43 million. Man

million over the first three years.
In net present value (NPV), the scenario of complete outsourcing was valued at $100 million, while the internal cost takeout scenario
was valued at $43 million. Management decided to perform only the internal cost takeout program since it required only a modest
investment and did not carry any significant risk to the busi-ness. Although the cost reduction initiatives seemed difficult to execute
at first, they turned out to be very achievable for the technology leads, as opposed to the outsourcing options.

17Product managementis the generic term used by marketing professionals to characterize the set of processes required to organize and modify the scope and nature
of what an organization produces and markets. As indicated in section 9, product management for IT organizations requires a proactive, systematic approach that
takes into account user demand, the overall environment of the firm in the IT organization and client domains, including balancing the requirements dictated by
the firm strategy, and internal producti

text:
takes into account user demand, the overall environment of the firm in the IT organization and client domains, including balancing the requirements dictated by
the firm strategy, and internal production constraints. Since an IT organization produces a set of services and solutions, the temptation is great to use the term
services managementinstead. Unfortunately, services management in IT refers to the overall set of activities required to deliver services to users. Service management
includes activitiesas diverse as IT asset, fault, change, demand or service-level agreement management. It also includes activities related to the definition of the catalog
of services deliveredto end users, something also found in product management as defined in this paper. Also, be aware that the term producttends to be inter-
preted by technologists as a package application. The term product managementis used in this paper despite those ambiguities.

text:
CASE STUDY 2
PRINCIPLES, STANDARDS AND IMPLEMENTATION CONSIDERATIONS FOR ARCHITECTING
A NEW COMPENSATION PROGRAM
Consider the implementation of a new compensation program (CP) aimed at introducing variable compensation
within a population of executives who are currently paid using a mostly nonvariable plan. In architecting the new CP,
the organization has to adopt three perspectives (or “views”):
1. Conceptual.In order to make sure that the plan accomplishes the business objectives, the new compensation plan
architects have to make sure that they spell out the key principles that will be used in designing the plan. In this particular
case, some of the principles include (note: the following is not an exhaustive list):
• Transparency.In order to promote fairness and meritocracy, the compensation of all executives will be known to all,
as well as reviewed periodically by a group of their peers.
• Consistency. The CP will take into account the key level at which activities are performed by the executives (C-level,
corporate managers and line managers) and their actual span of control, managed by business or budget, or other
appropriate metrics that reflect the magnitude of the respon

chunk_id: /Users/manuelbarbero/Developer/AIStudio/data/corpora/demo/uploads/Architecting the IT Organization.pdf::page-22::chunk-81
doc_id: /Users/manuelbarbero/Developer/AIStudio/data/corpora/demo/uploads/Architecting the IT Organization.pdf
source_path: /Users/manuelbarbero/Developer/AIStudio/data/corpora/demo/uploads/Architecting the IT Organization.pdf
text:
by the executives (C-level,
corporate managers and line managers) and their actual span of control, managed by business or budget, or other
appropriate metrics that reflect the magnitude of the responsibility supported.
• Partner-like model.Both the fixed and variable parts of the compensation will be evaluated using an internal currency
(“unit”) whose value will fluctuate over time as a function of the overall enterprise’s results. In addition, individual
executives will be awarded an initial set of units and then additional units as a function of current pay level (for the
fixed part of the compensation) and ongoing performance (for the variable part).
• Promoting ownership.The variable part of compensation will be available optionally as a combination of cash and
stock, with stock being awarded at a discount to promote stock ownership.
2. Logical.Implementing the new CP calls for the specific definition of the constructs, that is the standards that will
be used, for example, with respect to the four items above. The plan will make specific provisions such as:
• Setting up the compensation committees.In order to implement the bold principles described above, the logical
model of

chunk_id: /Users/manuelbarbero/Developer/AIStudio/data/corpora/demo/uploads/Architecting the IT Organization.pdf::page-22::chunk-82
doc_id: /Users/manuelbarbero/Developer/AIStudio/data/corpora/demo/uploads/Architecting the IT Organization.pdf
source_path: /Users/manuelbarbero/Developer/AIStudio/data/corpora/demo/uploads/Architecting the IT Organization.pdf
text:
spect to the four items above. The plan will make specific provisions such as:
• Setting up the compensation committees.In order to implement the bold principles described above, the logical
model of the CP will have to spell out the membership rules and operating model of the one or various committees
that will actually set up and review how much units are awarded.
• Setting up a banding model.To achieve the bold principles, specific rules need to be agreed on to define the
“level” and “magnitude” of the responsibility to be recognized by the CP. For example, by considering three levels
(line, management and executive) and four types of magnitude (for example, budget under control of executives
in support functions or sales for those in charge of specific delivery), the compensation of all executives will be
mapped into a three by four matrix with distinct fixed/variable compensation schemes.
3. Implementation considerations.Finally, the CP needs to specify the details of the model, for example, defining
the specific boundaries of the bands defined in the compensation matrix. For example, line-level executives in charge
of sales of $2 million to $5 million should be compensated be

chunk_id: /Users/manuelbarbero/Developer/AIStudio/data/corpora/demo/uploads/Architecting the IT Organization.pdf::page-23::chunk-83
doc_id: /Users/manuelbarbero/Developer/AIStudio/data/corpora/demo/uploads/Architecting the IT Organization.pdf
source_path: /Users/manuelbarbero/Developer/AIStudio/data/corpora/demo/uploads/Architecting the IT Organization.pdf
text:
or example, defining
the specific boundaries of the bands defined in the compensation matrix. For example, line-level executives in charge
of sales of $2 million to $5 million should be compensated between $150,000 and $200,000, and so on for each of
the 12 elements of the matrix.
The case study illustrates that a new governance model has to be used for the definition and management of the new CP,
recognizing the distinction between:
• Principles, set by executive management and the board.
• Standards, discussed and developed in collaboration with the stakeholders (i.e., the executives concerned).
• Implementation considerations, mostly managed by the human resources department.

the IT group are vis-à-vis solutions and services that could be offered through other means (i.e., outsourcing or by the business unit itself).

the business units (see below) and alternatives in terms of capital investment and return on investment on the overall project.

I
t used to be that there would be no more difficult challenge for technologists and their enterprise
clients than to agree on a common IT strategy—to the point that, to the untrained eye, most financial
services organizations would look like they clearly function without an explicit IT strategy. Sure, one
can always find published high-l

chunk_id: /Users/manuelbarbero/Developer/AIStudio/data/corpora/demo/uploads/FS Journal, Edited by M-1. Barbero - Strategy and Architecture Concepts and Techniques - 2006.pdf::page-1::chunk-1
doc_id: /Users/manuelbarbero/Developer/AIStudio/data/corpora/demo/uploads/FS Journal, Edited by M-1. Barbero - Strategy and Architecture Concepts and Techniques - 2006.pdf
source_path: /Users/manuelbarbero/Developer/AIStudio/data/corpora/demo/uploads/FS Journal, Edited by M-1. Barbero - Strategy and Architecture Concepts and Techniques - 2006.pdf
text:
trategy—to the point that, to the untrained eye, most financial
services organizations would look like they clearly function without an explicit IT strategy. Sure, one
can always find published high-level statements regarding the mission of IT, mostly as “the set of tech-
nical activities enabling and supporting the business objectives,” but little more beyond that.
In our view, the manifest proof that an IT organization has a strategy is its ability to produce and maintain
the underlying technology frameworks—principles, standards and implementation blueprints—that
bring this strategy to life. In this context, a level of difficulty arises from the fact that financial services
organizations are best described as multi-enterprise organizations consisting of related, but often very
distinct, lines of business. So trying to develop a common enterprisewide IT strategy and architecture is
indeed a challenge for what some modern commentators would describe as a loosely connected con-
federation of warring tribes, one that merger and acquisition activity constantly throws into a frenzy of
initiatives aimed at reorganization, consolidation and rationalization.
Challenging, yes. Impossible,

chunk_id: /Users/manuelbarbero/Developer/AIStudio/data/corpora/demo/uploads/FS Journal, Edited by M-1. Barbero - Strategy and Architecture Concepts and Techniques - 2006.pdf::page-1::chunk-2
doc_id: /Users/manuelbarbero/Developer/AIStudio/data/corpora/demo/uploads/FS Journal, Edited by M-1. Barbero - Strategy and Architecture Concepts and Techniques - 2006.pdf
source_path: /Users/manuelbarbero/Developer/AIStudio/data/corpora/demo/uploads/FS Journal, Edited by M-1. Barbero - Strategy and Architecture Concepts and Techniques - 2006.pdf
text:
ion of warring tribes, one that merger and acquisition activity constantly throws into a frenzy of
initiatives aimed at reorganization, consolidation and rationalization.
Challenging, yes. Impossible, certainly not.
In fact, we believe that the development of IT strategy and its twin activity, the definition of IT architecture,
have never been more a matter of focus and interest within the financial services industry. We are living
through an architecture renaissance, a theme echoed in the lead article of this issue, because in the words
of Paul McDonnel, who leads our Insurance Industry practice: “Architecture is not optional. Everyone will
have one. It’s a matter of whether the architecture is pursued explicitly or if it becomes implicit. And for a
modern IT organization to be effective and achieve an appropriate maturity level, architecture is notan option.
There is no other way to define scope, perform planning and achieve alignment with the business.”
This said, here is what the world does notneed: another article extolling the merits of service-oriented
architectures. Instead, the technologist readers of the Financial Services Technology Journalhave told us they
need supporti

chunk_id: /Users/manuelbarbero/Developer/AIStudio/data/corpora/demo/uploads/FS Journal, Edited by M-1. Barbero - Strategy and Architecture Concepts and Techniques - 2006.pdf::page-1::chunk-3
doc_id: /Users/manuelbarbero/Developer/AIStudio/data/corpora/demo/uploads/FS Journal, Edited by M-1. Barbero - Strategy and Architecture Concepts and Techniques - 2006.pdf
source_path: /Users/manuelbarbero/Developer/AIStudio/data/corpora/demo/uploads/FS Journal, Edited by M-1. Barbero - Strategy and Architecture Concepts and Techniques - 2006.pdf
text:
orld does notneed: another article extolling the merits of service-oriented
architectures. Instead, the technologist readers of the Financial Services Technology Journalhave told us they
need supportin making their business colleagues understand the value of an IT strategy, along with help
in actually drafting their strategy. Non-technologists want to understand how they can indeed be part of
the process.
We are glad to oblige. In this issue, we gather a collection of papers that aim to promote the notion that
defining IT strategy and architecture is the right thing to do, that it can be done systematically, and that
tools and techniques are at the right state of maturity to support effective collaboration between the IT
architects and the business architects.
So if you are trying to sell your management on the need to budget efforts toward developing an IT
strategy and architecture, this issue is designed to be casually left on the desks of unsuspecting executives
who, lured by the attractive cover, will hopefully find themselves pulled into the heretofore mysterious
world of IT strategy and architecture and soon afterwards will embrace your budget requests.
Good reading!
Manuel B

tractive cover, will hopefully find themselves pulled into the heretofore mysterious
world of IT strategy and architecture and soon afterwards will embrace your budget requests.
Good reading!
Manuel Barbero
Executive Editor, BearingPoint’s Financial Services Technology Journal
A personnel and personal note: This is the first issue without Pierre Pureur at the helm. Pierre was our found-
ing editor and he is now back in the industry, applying his wisdom to a single client, his new employer, instead
of the many clients he supported over his eight years at BearingPoint. While here, among many other things,
he helped launch this journal, and he served as an immensely helpful guide to me and many others on all
things technology. We wish him the best and will continue to feature his insights in these pages.

In a previous article, we defined a frame-
work for defining an enterprise architecture
notation. The proposed notational frame-
work leveraged work done in the industry
in the areas of UML and architectural views.
As practicing consultants, we need pragmatic
tools that enable us to be more efficient
and effective while defining and building
enterprise architectures. In this article, we
aim to make the notational framework
more actionable by focusing on two areas:
describing physical architectures and styles.

Application to IT organizational design.
Fundamentally, organizational constructs,
processes and tasks, supporting tools,
metrics, standards, and principles can be
considered views of the overall model that
need to be implemented with the appro-
priate tools on the right infrastructure and
consistent with the organization’s overall
culture and value system. Other relevant
views of the model also include governance,
reporting, and roles and responsibilities
(Figure 3).
Summary of key ideas:
• IT solutions and IT organizational models
are too complex to be considered as a
whole. Approaching their design through
various angles or views helps manage
complexity. Views are interrelated but
self-contained.
Just like complex objects need to be observed from various angles
to be fully understood, an organizational architecture

17Product managementis the generic term used by marketing professionals to characterize the set of processes required to organize and modify the scope and nature
of what an organization produces and markets. As indicated in section 9, product management for IT organizations requires a proactive, systematic approach that
takes into account user demand, the overall environment of the firm in the IT organization and client domains, including balancing the requirements dictated by
the firm strategy, and internal production constraints. Since an IT organization produces a set of ser

text:
firm in the IT organization and client domains, including balancing the requirements dictated by
the firm strategy, and internal production constraints. Since an IT organization produces a set of services and solutions, the temptation is great to use the term
services managementinstead. Unfortunately, services management in IT refers to the overall set of activities required to deliver services to users. Service management
includes activitiesas diverse as IT asset, fault, change, demand or service-level agreement management. It also includes activities related to the definition of the catalog
of services deliveredto end users, something also found in product management as defined in this paper. Also, be aware that the term producttends to be inter-
preted by technologists as a package application. The term product managementis used in this paper despite those ambiguities.

CASE STUDY 2
PRINCIPLES, STANDARDS AND IMPLEMENTATION CONSIDERATIONS FOR ARCHITECTING
A NEW COMPENSATION PROGRAM
Consider the implementation of a new compensation program (CP) aimed at introducing variable compensation
within a population of executives who are

chunk_id: /Users/manuelbarbero/Developer/AIStudio/data/corpora/demo/uploads/FS Journal, Edited by M-1. Barbero - Strategy and Architecture Concepts and Techniques - 2006.pdf::page-40::chunk-155

text:
RATIONS FOR ARCHITECTING
A NEW COMPENSATION PROGRAM
Consider the implementation of a new compensation program (CP) aimed at introducing variable compensation
within a population of executives who are currently paid using a mostly nonvariable plan. In architecting the new CP,
the organization has to adopt three perspectives (or “views”):
1. Conceptual.In order to make sure that the plan accomplishes the business objectives, the new compensation plan
architects have to make sure that they spell out the key principles that will be used in designing the plan. In this particular
case, some of the principles include (note: the following is not an exhaustive list):
• Transparency.In order to promote fairness and meritocracy, the compensation of all executives will be known to all,
as well as reviewed periodically by a group of their peers.
• Consistency. The CP will take into account the key level at which activities are performed by the executives (C-level,
corporate managers and line managers) and their actual span of control, managed by business or budget, or other
appropriate metrics that reflect the magnitude of the responsibility supported.
• Partner-like model.Both the fixed and va

chunk_id: /Users/manuelbarbero/Developer/AIStudio/data/corpora/demo/uploads/FS Journal, Edited by M-1. Barbero - Strategy and Architecture Concepts and Techniques - 2006.pdf::page-40::chunk-156
doc_id: /Users/manuelbarbero/Developer/AIStudio/data/corpora/demo/uploads/FS Journal, Edited by M-1. Barbero - Strategy and Architecture Concepts and Techniques - 2006.pdf
source_path: /Users/manuelbarbero/Developer/AIStudio/data/corpora/demo/uploads/FS Journal, Edited by M-1. Barbero - Strategy and Architecture Concepts and Techniques - 2006.pdf
text:
rs) and their actual span of control, managed by business or budget, or other
appropriate metrics that reflect the magnitude of the responsibility supported.
• Partner-like model.Both the fixed and variable parts of the compensation will be evaluated using an internal currency
(“unit”) whose value will fluctuate over time as a function of the overall enterprise’s results. In addition, individual
executives will be awarded an initial set of units and then additional units as a function of current pay level (for the
fixed part of the compensation) and ongoing performance (for the variable part).
• Promoting ownership.The variable part of compensation will be available optionally as a combination of cash and
stock, with stock being awarded at a discount to promote stock ownership.
2. Logical.Implementing the new CP calls for the specific definition of the constructs, that is the standards that will
be used, for example, with respect to the four items above. The plan will make specific provisions such as:
• Setting up the compensation committees.In order to implement the bold principles described above, the logical
model of the CP will have to spell out the membership rules and operati

chunk_id: /Users/manuelbarbero/Developer/AIStudio/data/corpora/demo/uploads/FS Journal, Edited by M-1. Barbero - Strategy and Architecture Concepts and Techniques - 2006.pdf::page-40::chunk-157
doc_id: /Users/manuelbarbero/Developer/AIStudio/data/corpora/demo/uploads/FS Journal, Edited by M-1. Barbero - Strategy and Architecture Concepts and Techniques - 2006.pdf
source_path: /Users/manuelbarbero/Developer/AIStudio/data/corpora/demo/uploads/FS Journal, Edited by M-1. Barbero - Strategy and Architecture Concepts and Techniques - 2006.pdf
text:
visions such as:
• Setting up the compensation committees.In order to implement the bold principles described above, the logical
model of the CP will have to spell out the membership rules and operating model of the one or various committees
that will actually set up and review how much units are awarded.
• Setting up a banding model.To achieve the bold principles, specific rules need to be agreed on to define the
“level” and “magnitude” of the responsibility to be recognized by the CP. For example, by considering three levels
(line, management and executive) and four types of magnitude (for example, budget under control of executives
in support functions or sales for those in charge of specific delivery), the compensation of all executives will be
mapped into a three by four matrix with distinct fixed/variable compensation schemes.
3. Implementation considerations.Finally, the CP needs to specify the details of the model, for example, defining
the specific boundaries of the bands defined in the compensation matrix. For example, line-level executives in charge
of sales of $2 million to $5 million should be compensated between $150,000 and $200,000, and so on for each of
the 12 elem

chunk_id: /Users/manuelbarbero/Developer/AIStudio/data/corpora/demo/uploads/FS Journal, Edited by M-1. Barbero - Strategy and Architecture Concepts and Techniques - 2006.pdf::page-41::chunk-158

text:
ned in the compensation matrix. For example, line-level executives in charge
of sales of $2 million to $5 million should be compensated between $150,000 and $200,000, and so on for each of
the 12 elements of the matrix.
The case study illustrates that a new governance model has to be used for the definition and management of the new CP,
recognizing the distinction between:
• Principles, set by executive management and the board.
• Standards, discussed and developed in collaboration with the stakeholders (i.e., the executives concerned).
• Implementation considerations, mostly managed by the human resources department.

Figure 2. A Pattern Portal
Wiki provides a simplified HTML editor
that makes it easy to create new patterns
and add to existing ones. A Wiki Web page
can be changed simply by hitting the edit
button. Over time, the site and the patterns
improve. New Wiki Web pages are added
through links to existing ones. All an archi-
tecture team needs to use Wiki methodology
is Wiki software (which is free), a HTTP
server and Wiki-user documentation.
Using Wiki, many people can easily contri-
bute to patterns. Patterns can be added
to, expanded and revised by any authorized
architect. Key questions, such as the pattern
problem and context, capture the intent
of the pattern. Metadata is captured to allow
searches across problem spaces. Because
the pattern history is saved, editors can
easily be traced for questions.

Al at Jefferies and Beyond - Some Reflections
DECEMBER 2025
This document presents an analysis of Agentic Al, its evolution, current capabilities, limitations, and strategic implications
specifically for Jefferies and from the perspective of the TI group. It explores the transition of Al from descriptive to
generative and now to agentic forms, emphasizing the integration of reasoning, planning, and autonomous execution
across multiple modalities and outlines Jefferies' vision for embedding Al into its knowledge productivity ecosystem, the
expected future trajectory of Al, and societal impacts.
Al Agentic Al promises a significant advancement beyond earlier Al forms by purportedly combining perception,
reasoning, planning, and autonomous task execution to achieve high-level goals. Unlike traditional descriptive Al, which
focuses on statistical and algebraic methods for classification and prediction, and generative Al, which creates novel
content, agentic Al aims at orchestrating complex workflows autonomously across modalities such not just as numbers
and text but also images, and sound. This evolution expands Al's cognitive capabilities from memorization and
classificat

text:
ting complex workflows autonomously across modalities such not just as numbers
and text but also images, and sound. This evolution expands Al's cognitive capabilities from memorization and
classification ot generation and strategizing, enabling automation of entire workflows rather than isolated tasks.
Despite its promise, Agentic Al currently faces significant limitations. Its implementation still depends on simplified,
rule-based workflows such as decision trees and still lacks human-like common sense, intuition, and nuanced contextual
understanding. Al systems struggle with non-literal language, ambiguous scenarios, and unforeseen complexities. The
scarcity of high-quality, comprehensive data constrains training more autonomous systems. Furthermore, larger Al
models risk overfitting and reduced generalizability, while many lack formal mathematical rigor and explainability, which
is critical for trust ni high-stakes domains like finance and healthcare. Organizational inertia and risks of reinforcing
biases also complicate Al deployment. These factors restrict agentic Al's ability to fully replace complex human decision-
making at present.

Jefferies aims to leverage Al as a catalyst for reinventing knowledge work rather than treating it as a siloed
technology project. The firm's strategy emphasizes stakeholder-centered design, modularity, security, integration, and
human-Al collaboration. Al solutions are developed with input from content owners, technology teams, and end-users
to ensure usability and relevance. The approach balances speed-to-market with scalability, compliance, and human
oversight, deploying a mix of internal custom solutions (e.g., Madison for banking and research [1]), broad tools like
JefAl [2], and external vendor offerings [3]. Al assets are integrated as building blocks within a future-ready knowledge
ecosystem, accelerating a paradigm where Al augments human expertise and decision-making.
Overall, Al overall is expected to become abundant, ubiquitous, and cost-effective, lowering barriers for enterprise
end-users to build Al tools. However, on the supply-side, continuous market dominance by a few large providers is likely
due to high en

INTRODUCTION
Artificial Intelligence is entering a new phase: Agentic A.l The term has quickly populated the public discourse with
hundreds of videos, thought pieces, and vendor promotional materials, many of which repeat familiar points from
prior Al trends. Defining Agentic Al's surface goal is straightforward; understanding its strategic implications and
limitations requires deeper, more disciplined analysis to address several critical questions:
• What is genuinely new? How should we frame Agentic Al's emergence in relation to the broader trajectory
of Al's evolution?
• What can Al do

• Where does Agentic Al fit for Jefferies? How can Al —i ncluding Agentic Al —d eliver value ni our

• How does JefAl integrate into this vision? How can our initiative leverage recent developments in Al to

More broadly, the rise of Al as an epoch-defining phenomenon raises legitimate questions about its impact on the
skills —a nd more importantly, the capabilities —t hat will be expected of knowledge workers. While ti is tempting
to take a pessimistic view, history shows that radical advances ni knowledge and technology have produced
complex, mixed outcomes. The opportunities and challenges they bring must be reckoned with by all layers of
society - from individuals to organizations such as Jefferies, to society at large.

Cognitive Work and the Evolution of Al Modalities [4]
Cognitive work encompasses the spectrum of mental activities performed by the human brain. These activities can
be mapped along two axes: the modalities (numbers, language, images, and other representations) and the types
of cognitive processes. The main types include:
• Memorization: Storing and recalling information, such as remembering a picture or a fact. Humanity has
been able to rely electronics as a force multiplier on since the dawn of "Digitization Age," 70+ years ago.
• Classification: Categorizing inputs, e.g., identifying whether an animal is dangerous or safe. Al started
there based on mathematical advances in statistics and linear algebra since the early seventies.
• Manipulation: Transforming or reworking information, such as retelling a story or altering an image.
• Generation: Creating new artifacts, including original narratives, images, or sounds.
• Reasonin

text:
Manipulation: Transforming or reworking information, such as retelling a story or altering an image.
• Generation: Creating new artifacts, including original narratives, images, or sounds.
• Reasoning: Building logical inferences, such as deducing that animals with bigger claws are more
dangerous.
• Planning/Strategizing: Developing sequences of actions to achieve goals, like plotting a safe route through
a forest by avoiding dangerous animals.
These cognitive functions are not limited to a single modality; humans apply them to numbers, text, images,
sounds, and more. The last four cognitive processes have benefited from stupendous advances ni Al.
The Evolution of Al: From Descriptive to Generative to Agentic
• Descriptive (Traditional) Al
Early Al systems were designed to automate specific, well-defined cognitive tasks, primarily ni the domain
of numbers and structured data. These systems relied on rule-based logic and supervised learning to
perform tasks such as classification and prediction. Their scope was narrow, and they required human
intervention for adaptation.
• Generative Al
With advances ni deep learning and neural networks, Al evolved ot handle unstructured data and g

rediction. Their scope was narrow, and they required human
intervention for adaptation.
• Generative Al
With advances ni deep learning and neural networks, Al evolved ot handle unstructured data and generate
novel outputs. Generative Al models, such as GPT and DALL-E, can create text, images, audio, and code.
They learn from large datasets and use unsupervised or self-supervised learning ot produce added
content. This marked a significant expansion ni modality: Al can now operate on language, images, sound,
and more, moving beyond mere analysis to creative synthesis.
• Agentic Al
The latest leap si agentic A,l which should integrate perception, reasoning, planning, and autonomous
execution. Agentic Al systems are goal-oriented: given a high-level objective, they devise plans, orchestrate
tasks, and adapt dynamically across modalities. For example, an agentic Al tasked with optimizing taxes

Expansion of Modalities

Numbers: Computers, literally, have excelled at numerical analysis of structured data. Incidentally,
Generative and Agentic Al provides mixed results when ti comes to mathematics ni general: surprisingly
lackluster in terms of actual arithmetic and partner with exotic capabilities in terms of proof
development/verification [5][6]
Language: NLP models enabled understanding and generation of text.
• Images &S ound: Computer vision and audio models brought perception and generation to new sensory
domains.
• Multimodal Integration: Modern agentic Al can combine modalities, reasoning across text, images, and
sound to solve complex problems and automate sophisticated workflows.

The automation of cognitive work has progressed from simple descriptive analytics to generative creativity and now
to agentic autonomy. Each stage has broadened the scope of what machines can do, both in terms of cognitive
depth and the variety of modalities. Agentic Al represents the convergence of these capabilities, enabling
machines to function as intelligent agents that plan, reason, and execute across diverse domains.
This evolution is not just technological-it is fundamentally reshaping how organizations and individuals leverage
Al for productivity, creativity, and strategic decision-making.
B - THE STATE OF AI AND LATEST ADVANCES NI AGENTIC AI - LIMITATIONS
Agentic Al shifts the Al conversation from answering questions to making things happen." But there are intrinsic
limitations of the approach. Despite impressive advancements, Al systems, including agentic Al, are far from
achieving human-like reasoning or autonomous decision-making in complex, real-world contexts.
• Lack of Common Sense - Al's reasoning process is still largely linear, which hampers its ability to make
truly adaptive or creative decisions

text:
onomous decision-making in complex, real-world contexts.
• Lack of Common Sense - Al's reasoning process is still largely linear, which hampers its ability to make
truly adaptive or creative decisions that are essential in dynamic environments. This limitation stems from
the fundamental nature of current machine learning models and their reliance on training data, (which is,
as mentioned above) often insufficient in scope and quality — especially for complex tasks requiring
multiple senses or common-sense understanding.
• Al systems, even those with advanced language processing capabilities, often struggle with
understanding everyday concepts and situations, such as recognizing sarcasm or understanding implicit
meanings. Example: In a conversation, Al might respond to the phrase "Break a leg!" as if ti is a literal
instruction, rather than understanding it as an idiomatic expression conveying good luck.

automation etc..) for now still relies on simplified, predicate logic - We will continue to use workflows-

• Anything beyond these approaches requires a leap in capabilities that the current state of algorithms,
data quality, and computational power cannot support. For example, Al struggles with understanding
nuanced contexts or handling unforeseen scenarios with the sophistication of human reasoning and
experience - and as we will see in section E) - this is likely to be the case in the future.
Lack of Quality Data to Train Models - The development and deployment of agentic Al are constrained by
the very data and models available today. The volume of high-quality, comprehensive data needed to train
more advanced systems is scarce, which hampers progress toward more autonomous and reliable Al
solutions.
Al systems require vast amounts of high-quality data ot learn and improve. However, collecting and lab

Al systems require vast amounts of high-quality data ot learn and improve. However, collecting and labeling
such data can be expensive, time-consuming, and sometimes even impossible. For Example: Training an Al
to recognize and respond to various nuanced emotions in human faces might require collecting and
annotating thousands of images, which can be a significant challenge due to the vast differences in facial
expressions and cultural contexts.
At certain points, human labor remains more cost-effective than investing in the extensive data collection
and training necessary for truly advanced agentic Al.
Big is Big Now - But big might not be Better - The bigger the Al model, the more prone ti is to overfitting
and making mistakes. This limitation can lead to Al systems becoming too complex and losing their ability
to generalize and adapt to new situations.
• Lack of Formal Rigor and Explainability Many Al systems lack a formal mathematical framework, which
can make ti difficult to ensure their safety, security, and reliability. explaining Al decisions and reasoning,
especially in high-s

Example: A financial Al system designed to make trading decisions might not have a well-defined
mathematical model underlying its decision-making process, making it difficult to audit, validate, or correct
its performance. Another example would be that an Al-powered medical diagnosis system might struggle to
provide clear and understandable explanations for its decisions, which can lead to mistrust among
healthcare professionals or patients.
• LLM might actually be a dead-end in the evolution of Al - A new type of artificial intelligence is emerging
that is based on "world models," [7], systems designed to understand and predict the physical world rather

Predictive Architecture (JEPA), an architecture that learns by comparing abstract representations of data
rather than pixels or words. World models, which are expected to take up to a decade to fully mature, will
be far more suitable for physical applications like robotics and autonomous systems
In summary, while agentic Al is evolving, its current limitations include reliance on rule-based workflows, linear
reasoning, insufficient data quality, and meaningful change management hurdles — all of which restrict its ability
to perform autonomous, complex decision-making fully.

Al as a Catalyst for Reinventing Knowledge Work - At Jefferies, we view the adoption of Al not as a standalone
technology initiative, but as a foundational element in the reinvention of how knowledge workers operate. Our
approach is anchored ni a comprehensive conceptual model layered architecture that integrates technology, data,
user experience, governance, and workflow management.
Jefferies
6 Al at Jefferies | December 2025 DRAFT

Our model is built on several core principles, each guiding the design and deployment of Al across the firm:
• Stakeholder-Centered Design: Solutions are developed with input from all user groups-content owners,
technology teams, and end-users—to ensure relevance and usability.
• Modularity and Reuse: We build for reuse, creating standardized components that can be leveraged
across multiple domains and use cases.
• Security and Governance: Al is embedded within secure, monitored environments, wi

chunk_id: /Users/manuelbarbero/Developer/AIStudio/data/corpora/demo/uploads/trash/barbero_ai_whitepaper.pdf::page-7::chunk-19

text:
e: We build for reuse, creating standardized components that can be leveraged
across multiple domains and use cases.
• Security and Governance: Al is embedded within secure, monitored environments, with robust access
control and policy management (e.g., JefPolicy, OPA) that serve the entire ecosystem, not just Al-specific
functions.
Integration and Interoperability: Open APls and facades abstract complexity, allowing seamless
integration with existing systems and workflows.
• Human-Al Collaboration: We design for human-centered Al, ensuring that automation augments-not
replaces-human expertise, and that knowledge workers can co-create and manage knowledge with Al
agents.
The overall guiding principle is that we try, as much as possible, to map what we do to a component of the
conceptual model, with the explicit goal of either building foundational capabilities or accelerating their adoption.
A more complete set of design Principles can be found here [8].
From a tactical standpoint, achieving the goal will make a multiplicity of initiatives, some of them overlapping in
scope or capabilities depending on usage. In that respect, we continue to look at the overall environment as a
comb

"The universe is not infinite and even if it was, robot don't live in it" - To meet the "Verifier Law", i.e. to
be able to predict that a particular problem can be handled better, faster by leveraging Al, the solution need
to be trained on a large set of reliable data with outcome testable against some well determinable
definition of "what good looks like". We cannot ethically or otherwise perform 1,000,000 operations on that
many patients and learn what works best. This could not be a showstopper fi there was a way to simulate
how the human body reacts to physical handling. Well, the reality is that the state of the art in terms of
simulation at the cell-level is lagging immensely in terms of maturity when compared with say, modeling a
chess game. No simulation and no repeated experiment mean no training, thus limited Al-powered
solutions

text:
al Universe is Big - But Nature is Even Bigger" [11] - Recent estimates place the total data
created, captured, copied, and consumed globally in 2023 at roughly 120 zettabytes — that's 1.2×10 23
bytes. Much of this is ephemeral or heavily duplicated; when narrowed to stored, unique, and indexable
digital knowledge accessible via the web, credible analyses suggest a scale closer to 1014 to 1015 bytes,
depending on definition and inclusion criteria.
fI we apply a (admittedly somewhat) generous upper-bound model for the human brain's sustained
knowledge intake — about 1 megabyte per second of meaningful sensory and linguistic input during active
wakefulness (the effective bandwidth of the optimal nerve) - then across 16 waking hours, this translates
to roughly 5.8×101º bytes per day. Over a five-year span, that total approaches 10 ' bytes of processed
input, placing it ni the same order of magnitude as the indexed corpus of human digital knowledge.
To be clear, this is not a claim about raw storage equivalence. Human cognition compresses, filters, and
integrates experience — retaining concepts, relationships, and procedural skills while discarding most raw
sensory detail. Thus, the fu

chunk_id: /Users/manuelbarbero/Developer/AIStudio/data/corpora/demo/uploads/trash/barbero_ai_whitepaper.pdf::page-10::chunk-29
doc_id: /Users/manuelbarbero/Developer/AIStudio/data/corpora/demo/uploads/trash/barbero_ai_whitepaper.pdf
source_path: /Users/manuelbarbero/Developer/AIStudio/data/corpora/demo/uploads/trash/barbero_ai_whitepaper.pdf
text:
aw storage equivalence. Human cognition compresses, filters, and
integrates experience — retaining concepts, relationships, and procedural skills while discarding most raw
sensory detail. Thus, the functional knowledge base after five years would be far smaller in byte terms than
the digital corpus, yet arguably richer in semantic and contextual depth. This highlights how humans and
machines transform data differently: machines ingest vast quantities quickly but often without deep
contextual grounding, whereas humans absorb more slowly but integrate knowledge tightly with lived
experience. fI we accept that knowledge emerges from data, filtered through interpretation and synthesis,
this comparison sharply contextualizes what even the largest digital systems can effectively "know."
Human acquisition is slower but profoundly integrated into lived context, something digital systems still
struggle to replicate.
• Specialized vs "General" Intelligence — in Humans and Machines - Machines have exhibited superiority
over humans in specialized tasks for decades - from early mechanical calculators to modern protein-
folding models. The idea of Al surpassing humans in certain well-structured

nor alarming. But human intelligence itself si highly specialized —a n evolutionary toolkit honed for

10 Al at Jefferies | December 2025 DRAFT

• When we strip away the marketing veneer, "Artificial General Intelligence" collapses into a collection
of domain-specific optimizations, each thriving under certain conditions. The Verifier's Law offers a
predictive lens: Al will continue expanding into domains meeting the verifiability criteria —a bundant data,
cheap experimentation, unambiguous correctness measures — and will remain vastly better than humans
there. Conversely, humans will retain superiority in domains characterized by sparse data, ill-structured
objectives, and high contextual variability - areas where judgement and creativity emerge from deeply
embodied experience, not from statistical optim

E - CONCLUSION(S?)
Humanity's intellectual journey can be imagined as a tiered pyramid - from collecting raw data ni ancient times,
to shaping it into information through numerical systems and ledgers, ot recording knowledge ni books and later
digital form. The leap to insight, enabled by breakthroughs such as calculus, allowed us ot reach higher, revealing
the laws governing the universe and general insights.
The acceleration brought by digitization and mathematical progress has compressed the timelines for reaching
each tier — yet at the summit remains wisdom and judgement, historically the preserve of human minds. Whether
Al can fully ascend into this domain is still contested: machines, limited by their disembodied nature, may struggle
to capture the non-textual, ephemeral, sensory threads of understanding woven into human decision-making.
Bridging this gap, i.e. constantly exposing Al systems through real t

text:
ied nature, may struggle
to capture the non-textual, ephemeral, sensory threads of understanding woven into human decision-making.
Bridging this gap, i.e. constantly exposing Al systems through real time data acquisition (think Meta or Google foray
into smart glasses) might help bridge that gap. But this general, real-time transparency could also create a
dystopian reality (as narrated ni The Circle by Dave Eggers [12])
And yet, examples from our own practice challenge any simple dichotomy. In considering the recent rebranding of
Metis into JefAl with Jefferies: a handful of prompts produced an entire campaign structure — themes, slogans,
positioning - within minutes. But ti was not Al that recognized the strategic necessity for rebranding; that
judgement came from lived experience; the kind Bill Duggan describes as Strategic Intuition [13], i.e. "knowing that
we needed rebranding" was not an Al-generated idea.
Overall, synthesis of machine speed and human vision is not just a capability — it is a competitive differentiator for
organization and people. The challenge ahead is uneven distribution of benefits across knowledge worker. Drawing
from Elliott Jaques' Stratified Systems The

text:
ty — it is a competitive differentiator for
organization and people. The challenge ahead is uneven distribution of benefits across knowledge worker. Drawing
from Elliott Jaques' Stratified Systems Theory [14] only a fraction — 10-40% — of high-stratum thinkers will harness
Al to deliver work at ten times speed and quality. The remainder will see much of their contribution commoditized,
their economic relevance diminished by factors of two times, four times, or ten times. This will widen the gulf
between the Al-literate and Al-illiterate, a divide with profound market and societal consequences.
Do we worry about our children? Yes —fi we neglect Al literacy at scale. No —fi we commit to embedding Al
fluency in education, career entry, and lifelong skill development. Calls to cease hiring junior talent on the grounds
that Al can replicate their work ignore the deeper truth: wisdom and judgement, the apex of the pyramid, cannot
simply be engineered, it is learned through practice. fI we stop cultivating these skills now, we will have no leaders
with the capacity to guide decision-making in a decade's time.

But alternative futures are possible. As Yuval Noah Harari [15] warns, the displacement of broad swathes of
knowledge workers could birth a "Useless Class" - while demand for manual and service roles stabilizes or rises.
Whether this trigger leads ot social innovations like Universal Basic Income or ot more troubling methods of
pacification is unknown.
The question is not simply whether Al will reach the summit of human intellect — it is who will be there when it
happens. fI wisdom and judgement remain human strengths, they will do so because we deliberately choose to
cultivate them, preparing minds capable of steering society through an age where intelligence is abundant, but
meaning is scarce.
The future is not Al replacing human judgement — ti is strategic co-design of workflows where machines scale
and humans decide. Jefferies opportunity lies in building systems that harness both strengths, continually
updating our architecture, and preparing our people for deep Al fluency.
Rather than a binary dis

text:
humans decide. Jefferies opportunity lies in building systems that harness both strengths, continually
updating our architecture, and preparing our people for deep Al fluency.
Rather than a binary displacement, the trajectory of Al and human intelligence looks like a mutual expansion of
specialized domains. Machines will take over more of the structured, repeatable work; humans will continue to
dominate the ambiguous, high-context decisions where verifiability is not possible.
For organizations like Jefferies, this reality underscores the design principle for Al integration: pair machine
strength with human judgement, do not attempt to force one to replace the other.
• Accelerate deployment in high-verifiability domains (quant, compliance, structured market research).
• Actively invest in exploration workflows that might break into low-verifiability territories over time.
• Re-engineer operational models to make Al a process engine rather than a decorative tool, accepting
that some builds will be short-lived.
Maintain a market-wide lens, anticipating where Al will uplift sectors, displace labor, and trigger
regulatory or societal shifts that could reshape investment landscapes.
